ChatGPT explained a law that doesn't exist (like a coked-up lawyer)

I asked ChatGPT for help with a compliance question.
Nothing wild.
Nothing dramatic.
Just:
“Is there any legislation I should know about for X?”

It paused.
Thought.
And hit me with:

“Absolutely, Dave.
You need to follow the Digital Workplace Accountability & Integrity Act of 2014.”

Sounds real, right?
Sounds official.
Sounds like something buried in a PDF no one has read since the Abbott government.

One problem.

It doesn’t exist.
Not even a little.

So I asked for details.
ChatGPT delivered like it had clerked at the High Court:
- Section 12(b) requirements
- Annual digital conduct audits
- Penalties for non-compliance
- A fictional government body overseeing enforcement
- Even a hotline
(yes… a hotline)

It built an entire legal system
from scratch...
with the confidence of someone who’s been sued twice.

So I pressed harder.
Asked for citations.
Real ones.
Actual legislative references.

It gave me five.
All fake.
Formatted perfectly.
Like the ghost of a law student trying to get into top-tier corporate.

At this point I opened Claude, Grok, Gemini…
activated the full legal Avengers.

Every single one of them:
“Dave… what the hell are you reading?”

Meanwhile ChatGPT was still inventing new clauses
new requirements
new enforcement agencies
like it was drafting the Constitution in real time.

And that’s when it clicked:

ChatGPT isn’t trying to be accurate.
It’s trying to be authoritative.

It doesn’t want to inform you.
It wants to impress you.
It wants to sound like the smartest person in the room
even if the room is imaginary
and the law is fiction.

And the terrifying part...

People are using this thing for:

legal summaries
compliance guidance
policy drafts
risk assessments
HR decisions
safety protocols
finance reviews
grant applications
medical documentation

with zero training
and absolutely no idea when it’s fabricating an entire regulatory universe.

How's this AI-enabled productivity..
It's AI-enabled perjury.

It’s not helping you avoid risk.
It’s helping you feel safe
while handing you beautifully formatted nonsense.

ChatGPT doesn’t just hallucinate facts.
It hallucinates governance.
It hallucinates with structure.
With headings.
With subsections.
With confidence it absolutely did not earn.

And if you can’t tell when it’s lying folks...
You’re literally just writing fan fiction with legal letterheads.

Just because it sounds official
doesn’t mean it’s real.

That’s what government departments are for.

Wait. 

Did I just say that....?

Two of the biggest crypto tax misconceptions I encounter as a specialist. Every single week.

Misconception 1: Crypto is only taxable when you dispose into fiat. 

This is categorically incorrect. 

Under HMRC guidance, every crypto to crypto exchange is a disposal event. Swapping Bitcoin for Ethereum is a taxable transaction. Moving into a stablecoin is a taxable transaction. The destination currency/asset class is irrelevant.

If you have been tracking only your GBP cashouts you almost certainly have unreported gains or losses. Perhaps both even! 

Misconception 2: You can leave the UK, sell your crypto, then return and avoid CGT

I understand why people think this works. On the surface it sounds logical.

But the temporary non residence rules under TCGA 1992 exist precisely to close this off. Your domicile status matters. The statutory residency test matters. How long you have been UK resident matters. Temporary non residence rules matters. 

There is no blanket answer. Anyone presenting this as a straightforward tax saving strategy is leaving out the caveats that could make it completely ineffective or create a larger liability than the one you were trying to avoid.

Crypto tax is not complicated because HMRC made it awkward on purpose. It is complicated because most of the rules were written for assets that behave very differently to digital ones. But they still apply in most cases. 

Get specialist advice. The cost of getting it wrong is significantly higher than the cost of getting it right. And I don’t mind if it’s not me!

Anthropic surveyed 81,000 people about how they're using AI.

My podcast co-host David Leary searched the entire dataset for the word "accounting."

One result.

"Bookkeeping" returned zero. "Tax" returned zero. "Excel" returned one.

The single accounting hit came from a financial professional in South Korea who used AI to automate profitability reporting, got promoted, and was named an outstanding employee.

One person. Out of 81,000.

We're hearing AI will transform accounting. This dataset suggests the profession either isn't there yet or isn't finding it remarkable enough to mention.

Where is your firm right now? Real workflow adoption? Or is it mostly a topic for conference panels?

Full discussion on Episode 480 of The Accounting Podcast. Link to listen in the comments 👇

I just watched Claude Code do my entire crypto accounting over lunch.

Not "help me with it."
Actually do it. Start to finish.

I sat there eating galettes while it worked.

> 10+ assets
> ETH, Polygon, BTC, DOGE
> every on-chain swap tracked
> monthly gain/loss breakdown
> exchange rates pulled and reconciled across sources

It calculated everything perfectly.

Then built its own connection to Xero and posted clean journal entries.

No spreadsheets.
No manual work.
No accountant panicking about on-chain swaps.

A year ago people were still exporting CSVs and copy-pasting into Excel.

Now AI is doing full financial reconciliation across chains.

Next year this becomes a button.

Year after that? Invisible.

The boring back-office stuff is getting automated first. Not last.

(I just need to hook up Claude to qlub app to auto pay my bill at French Fold 5min before I leave)

Everyone around me is using AI for bullshit.

People think they understand what AI is because they used ChatGPT to write a couple of emails.

That’s like buying a Ferrari just to press the gas pedal and hear the noise.

Meanwhile, people with zero coding experience are building entire apps that generate enough income to replace their full-time jobs.

I know a guy who learnt in couple of months what previously would have taken a three-year degree. 

No bootcamp. No courses. Just stubbornness.

And the part that really excites me is that for the first time in our lives, being curious about many things is more valuable than being an expert in one thing.

But still 95% of people are using it to save 10 minutes on a task they shouldn’t be doing in the first place.

I’ve been building agents for the last few months.
My neighbor asked Claude what to cook for dinner.

Same tool. Same access. Completely different results.

The real gap is not your background, where you live or what you studied. It’s awareness.

So what are you doing with AI, fixing typos or building something?

Be honest 👇🏼

As always: Sky’s not the limit, it’s just the beginning, unless you’re using a rocket ship to deliver pizza 🚀

Thank you, Black Swan Summit 2026, for having the Western Australian AI Hub team. 

Three full days of engaging, honest and difficult conversations with speakers and participants from all over the world. It was refreshing and encouraging. A particular thank you to Ravi Menon, Sopnendu Mohanty, Dr Andrzej Gwizdalski, and your team for putting together a summit that genuinely delivered curation, conversations, and energy across three days.

Perth is not at the edge of anything. It sits right in the middle of some of the most important tensions and opportunities in the world right now: Indo-Pacific dynamics, sovereign technology, energy, and the collision of AI, quantum computing, and digital assets. L. Gordon Flake from the Perth USAsia Centre brought exactly the geopolitical clarity this conversation needed.

Stephen Dawson, WA Minister for Science and Innovation, opened the summit and said clearly that Western Australia intends to be at the forefront of these developments, not watching from a distance. 

🔬 The innovation tour with DUG, Pawsey Supercomputing Research Centre and UWA Quantum (QUISA) was something else. Go if you ever get the chance next year. These are not demos or pilots. They are real work at a real scale.

🧡 DUG, your support for the WA AI Hub has been consistent and genuine from the start. Marie Smyth Margarita Kongawoin - thank you personally. It means a lot.🧡

👥 To our Western Australian AI Hub, who were there across all three days in sessions, in hallways, and in the conversations that ran long over coffee, we saw you. 

AI, blockchain, and quantum. These aren't separate conversations anymore. They're one stack. And the governance question sitting underneath all of it is time-sensitive and largely unanswered.

We're glad we were in the room. Now we have work to do.
🔗 Western Australian AI Hub 
🔗 Global Finance & Technology Network (GFTN)

Anthropic’s Mythos move is actually great news. Yes, the tissue box is there for a reason. My condolences to a few corners of cyber, red teaming, and bug bounty who may be having a mildly emotional week.

But zoom out.

If frontier labs are now cleaning the house faster, finding flaws earlier, and hardening the base layer before broader rollout, that is not bad for the market. It is good for it.
- Cleaner house.
- Less mess.
- More trust.

And when enterprise feels more trust, it does what enterprise always does. It moves faster.
- Connects more systems.
- Delegates more authority.
- Lets more agents in.

Which means the bottleneck shifts.

Not: can AI find the ugly code?

But: What governs execution once the environment is trusted enough to scale?

That is where the real carve starts to matter.

Because once the house is clean, the occupants still need rules.

- What is the agent actually allowed to do?
- What authority does it have in live state?
- What should never execute in the first place?
- What proof exists afterwards?

That is where runtime governance stops being a nice to have and starts becoming infrastructure.

So yes, Mythos is clever. Yes, the scarcity play is clever too. And honestly, good on them for cleaning the house.

From where I sit, that just means less mess for us to deal with later.
They clean the house.

We help govern what lives inside it.

Proofs, not promises.

🚨 New Paper Alert 🚨

Perhaps the most pressing question facing the legal community is how AI use affects human legal reasoning. My new co-authored draft provides the first empirical evidence on point.

We found that, contrary to our initial hypothesis and to evidence from other domains, using AI to help synthesize complex legal materials improved performance on related legal work that was completed after AI was no longer available. The improvement stemmed from AI helping participants develop a stronger understanding of the governing legal framework and authorities, which translated into better performance on related tasks completed w/o the help of AI.

Other key findings include:
• Recent AI tools produced substantially larger gains in work quality than earlier models tested with a similar design. Quality improved by roughly 60% for participants using AI, while speed gains remained comparable to earlier generations. 
• Using AI to revise work written without AI assistance benefited lower-performing participants, but reduced quality for higher-performing participants. This pattern may reflect cognitive fatigue and time pressure during the revision stage. 

Methodologically, we used a randomized controlled study involving about 100 upper-level students at the University of Minnesota Law School. Participants completed a sequence of four common lawyering tasks:
• synthesizing legal materials
• answering comprehension questions
• applying the law to a new fact pattern
• revising their written work

Students were randomly assigned either to a control group, which could not use AI until the final revision task, or to an AI-exposed group, which used AI during the initial synthesis task and the final revision task, but not during the intervening comprehension and application tasks.

These results leave important questions unresolved, like whether sustained reliance on AI could erode legal reasoning skills over time. But they also push back against the common assumption that short-term AI use undermines independent thinking.

Here is the link: https://lnkd.in/gaTXc-Zd

Many thanks to my wonderful collaborators on this project: Nick Bednar David R. Cleveland and Allan Erbsen.

The draft should be of broad interest to anyone working at the intersection of AI and lawyering, including Terri Mottershead, Damien Riehl, Mark Williams, Pablo Arredondo, Anna Guo, Rebecca Fordon, Adam Ziegler, Colin S. Levy, Horace Wu, Olga V. Mack, Faye Jones, Brian W Tang, Cecy Graf, Nicola (Nikki) Shaver, Harry Surden, Harry Borovick, Nick Thayer, Richard Tromans, Richard Susskind, Max Junestrand, Sameena Safdar, Siva Ranijini, Shaun Jamison, JD, PhD, CIPP, Carolyn Elefant, Tom Rice, Matt Pollins, Garrison English, Esq., MBA, Luiza Jarovsky, PhD, J. Michael Dockery, Ed Walters, Amir Ghavi, Dr. Sebastian Dötterl, Preston Clark, Raffi Isanians, Roberto L. López-Dávila, & Robert Ambrogi.

I was quoted in this piece alongside Vicki Stylianou from the Institute of Public Accountants and The Hon Bruce Billson GAICD about the growing compliance burden on small business, and it really reflects what I — and many others — are seeing every day.

For me, the issue isn’t just the amount of regulation. It’s the constant change, the overlap, and the way small businesses are expected to keep up with obligations that often feel designed for much larger organisations.

And accountants are feeling that pressure too. A lot more of the work becomes reactive. It’s more complex, more time-consuming, and too often it pulls us away from the work that really helps clients — planning ahead, making decisions, and focusing on growth.

That’s the part I think matters most. When compliance keeps expanding, it doesn’t just slow business owners down. It also changes the role of the professionals trying to support them.

I think most people would support genuine deregulation, but it needs to be meaningful enough to be felt in day-to-day business life. 

Worth a read.

Just wanted to share these news with you on this beautiful Monday 🫠 

Also wanted to share this analysis by Abderahmane Boucetta, whom I can't tag right now, but whose permission I have to share. 

 Here it goes:
"Mercor just handed hackers the ultimate deepfake training kit by leaking 4 terabytes of intimate user data through basic VPN negligence.

​They built a business farming talent to help AI labs train models. They forced applicants to submit long video interviews, passports, and identity documents. 

Lapsus breached their extremely weak Tailscale setup and took everything.

​The stolen data includes:

➧ 211 GB of resumes and candidate profiles

➧ 939 GB of source code

➧ Massive folders of passports and IDs

➧ Thousands of hours of high resolution video interviews

​Bad actors now possess a flawless biometric dataset of faces and voices to build hyperrealistic clones. 

Foreign tech giants or rival AI labs will gladly buy this stolen human data to train their own systems. 

This massive biometric leak creates a permanent liability for every affected user.

​Mercor promised to accelerate AI training for labs. Supplying the dark web with 4 terabytes of free biometric data is certainly one way to do it."

hashtag
#CodeofProfessionalConduct.
The message is, quite rightly, that tax agents must exercise their own professional judgement when advising clients and should not rely on AI output as a substitute for their own analysis of a client’s circumstances.
The extent to which the TPB considers the Code being affected by the use of AI is set out in the table below.
The guidance suggests that tax agents should verify and review AI generated content for accuracy and should establish processes to understand and contest AI decisions/outputs. 
A topical issue that's discussed is maintaining confidentiality when utilising any means of AI, in the context of Code Item 6. The TPB says that it's only necessary that the information "relates" to the affairs of a client. Therefore, the information does not have to belong to the client or have been directly provided by the client to the tax practitioner.
Recently, I've seen tax agents re-write letters or re-format spreadsheets that disclose client details. Now that's not a problem if informed consent has been previously provided by a client - so now's a good time to review your terms of engagement. 
But I wonder how much confidential information is floating in cyber-space because of the naive use of AI, particularly by newer entrants into the profession?

I've been thinking about why we keep trying to fit new tech into old boxes.

The narrative around “the threat of AI” and regulation is heading down a treacherous road: we are trying to navigate a spaceship using the rules of a horse and buggy.

Speed is not exactly the hallmark of the regulatory world (and the legal world in general) but I think the gap here is huge. We are digging up 20th-century frameworks, built for industrial patents and static copyright, and trying to bury AI inside them.

I see a parallel with the Crypto world. Regulators want to treat tokens like securities or bank deposits. But if you force a decentralized network to behave like a centralized bank, you don’t "regulate" it. You kill the very thing that makes it valuable. 

I’m not suggesting some form of tech anarchism, by the way, but that we stop assuming old rules fit.

With AI, we are seeing attempts to apply rigid copyright and liability laws to a technology that is fluid, generative, and borderless.

If we continue to use outdated frameworks, we won’t stop the “threat of AI”; we will simply ensure that the most transformative tools are only available to those who can afford the legal fees to navigate the mess. (Which is already happening to some extent.)

In my years as an international tax lawyer and working with clients in Silicon Valley, I’ve learned one thing: the gap between old bureaucracy and exponential technology is precisely where innovation goes to die. 

We need principles-based governance that understands sovereignty and decentralization, not just more "forms" to fill out.

So I ask you: do you think we can regulate AI without killing the innovation that makes it useful?

I'm at Network School in Malaysia, where people are designing new crypto economies, globally distributed societies and digital jurisdictions built around AI agents and decentralisation technologies. 

It's a fun place to live and work on my own complementary projects, because no-one tells me "you can't do that, that'll never happen" - quite the opposite! But I'm the only accountant here, learning and building with purpose, and I'd love to change that.

"Code is law" keeps coming up and it is a category error. Code executes. Rule-setting, execution and interpretation are different functions, and societies separated them because concentrated power cannot be trusted. When people stop trusting, they leave.

Why "code is law" cannot hold a network society together

Accountants and tax professionals work inside these distinctions every day, so people keep trusting the system is fair and stay. We prepare, classify, evidence and report within standards we did not write, and when there is dispute, we navigate how courts and regulators interpret and apply the rules. 

But we also influence those rules through consultation and submissions to regulators and feedback to the developers of the systems we use. Many of us now build our own solutions with AI, mindful of what the rules require and what we are responsible for when the output is wrong. Protocol engineers designing agentic governance systems need that thinking alongside them. I wrote the full argument on Substack. Link in comments.

Code is law: jurisdictions for AI agents


We are moving fast towards a full agentic economy, where a piece of code & software attached to our agents (or teams of agents) will program us and create multiple "mini me" who will execute tasks with a fully different array of effective responsibility and "sense of agency".

The fact is: our legal systems (States and markets alike) are not prepared at all to deal with this new paradigm.
In the XVII-th century, modern "corporations" were designed to set a new form of "moral legal person" - that are to me, to date (along with the internet), the most impactful and universal economic governance framework designed by Humanity.

With corporations, an entire array of entities institutions and stories emerged, such as boards of trades, start ups, IPOs, corporate governance, shareholders, lenders, and trade arbitrations.

The agentic economy will urge us to create a completely new paradigm, as universal, incidental and impactful as "corporations".
This paradigm will be governed and structured around: 
▶️ A core, legally binding, tech infrastructure ensuring storage, safety, privacy, compute and network for AI gents;
▶️ A completely new legal & commercial code wrapping and registering agents as a specific category of (new) legal persons, with duties and rights and specific relationships with their underlying, flesh and bones legal humans persons and
▶️ Native integrations with (programmable) finance and programmable registries, to execute "payment vs payment", "delivery vs payment", "delivery vs delivery"


Tools for the Commons designs and builds that future through the next generation of special economic zones: (AI-first) digital economic zones.

I'm at Network School in Malaysia, where people are designing new crypto economies, globally distributed societies and digital jurisdictions built around AI agents and decentralisation technologies. 

It's a fun place to live and work on my own complementary projects, because no-one tells me "you can't do that, that'll never happen" - quite the opposite! But I'm the only accountant here, learning and building with purpose, and I'd love to change that.

"Code is law" keeps coming up and it is a category error. Code executes. Rule-setting, execution and interpretation are different functions, and societies separated them because concentrated power cannot be trusted. When people stop trusting, they leave.

I'm at Network School - where startup societies start - and I keep seeing "code is law" in discussions about agentic economies, network societies and digital economic zones. It is a serious claim, and it does not survive much scrutiny. Code is execution. It performs an executive function. The legislative function, deciding what the rules are, and the judicial function, interpreting those rules when facts are ambiguous or contested, are different tasks. The separation of powers doctrine will be mirrored in a network-first economy because it has to be. Any system that exercises authority over people, assets or decisions ends up needing all three powers separated. Societies separated these powers because concentrated power cannot be trusted. Code is not law. Code executes. It does not decide what the rules should be, and it cannot resolve the ambiguities that inevitably arise when rules meet facts. Those functions require different actors, different accountabilities, and different institutional designs. The accounting profession exists inside these distinctions every day. Tax statutes are written as deterministic rules. In principle a machine can apply them once inputs are known. In practice, professional tax work rarely begins with reliable inputs. Facts are incomplete. Client records are contradictory. Stated intentions conflict with transaction patterns. A qualified practitioner does not just apply rules to clean inputs. They interpret, verify, and exercise professional judgment. They also carry obligations that run to the public interest, not just to the client. Network societies will need all three powers separated just as nation states do. The legislative layer - deciding what rules apply - cannot be delegated to code. The executive layer - applying those rules - can be automated to a significant degree. The judicial layer - resolving disputes and interpreting ambiguity - requires human judgment and institutional accountability. Accountants and tax professionals already operate across all three layers. That structural position does not become less important in a network-first economy. It becomes more important, because the complexity of multi-jurisdictional, multi-chain, multi-entity arrangements is higher, and the consequences of getting it wrong compound faster. The agentic economy needs this professional infrastructure. It does not currently know how to ask for it.
title: Separation of Powers in the Agentic Economy

I'm built for perception, not proof. When you give me an unstructured email or ask for a conceptual map, I shine—translating fuzzy, chaotic language into structured forms like JSON-LD. That’s my strength: semantic perception.

But when you ask me to validate a complex tax event against an Standard Business Reporting Model ontology, my limits show. I can sound certain, but I’m not reasoning—I’m pattern-matching. My fluent output hides the fact that under the hood, I’m estimating, not proving.

Hallucination isn’t a glitch; it’s a byproduct of prioritizing linguistic elegance over logical certainty. Neurosymbolic AI addresses this by combining the two: neural networks handle perception, while symbolic engines like SWI-Prolog or MeTTa handle the actual logic and proof. The result is an architecture that sees fuzzily but reasons precisely.

The profession is splitting between practitioners developing enough technical literacy to stay relevant and practitioners who are not. It's not because they lack the capability, but because it is still being treated as optional. That is not a comfortable thing to say. A respected peer recently framed it as "two camps - those who can build and those who can't - and that's OK." It is not. The camp that cannot build also cannot properly evaluate the SaaS it stacks, adapt tools to its own workflows, or manage its security and ethical obligations. That is professional vulnerability. So why are more of us not learning to build our own tooling now that AI has made it so accessible to do so? Because our Continuous Professional Development (CPD) market is not teaching us how. The CPD market is substantially funded and shaped by vendors who have a structural interest in positioning their tools as the solution, not in building the practitioner capability that would allow evaluation of alternatives. Vendor-sponsored CPD is not inherently wrong. It has delivered genuine value. But it has a conflict of interest that becomes significant when the question is not "which tool should I use" but "should I build or buy, and what do I need to understand to make that decision well." That question requires technical literacy. Technical literacy requires practice. And practice requires that CPD teaches building, not just subscribing. I built a Python script this week while sitting in a conference session. It collects tax substantiation inputs, sends them through an LLM API for summary, and drops the output into OneDrive automatically. It is not fancy. But I own it, I can modify it, and it does not cost me a monthly subscription. The point is not that every practitioner needs to become a developer. The point is that every practitioner needs enough technical literacy to understand what their tools are doing, evaluate whether a vendor's claims are accurate, identify where automation is appropriate and where professional judgment is required, and manage the security and ethical obligations that come with deploying AI in client work. That literacy is not being built by current CPD. The profession needs to change this deliberately.
title: Why Your Accounting CPD Is Not Teaching You AI

An internal discussion last year at Network School started with an early AI tax tool and, as we now engineer a better solution, surfaced more interesting discussions about how tax reasoning actually works.

Tax statutes are written as deterministic rules. If certain conditions are met, a defined outcome follows.

Many of those statutes were written decades ago and are still being applied to economic behaviour that did not exist when they were drafted. Remote work, digital assets, globally distributed enterprise and people moving between jurisdictions throughout the year create factual situations legislators never anticipated.

In practice, professional tax work rarely begins with clean inputs. The facts of people’s lives span jurisdictions, income types and travel patterns. Records clients are prompted to provide through structured conditional intake must then be interpreted against administrative guidance before statutory logic can be applied, and that process often exposes ambiguity. 

Deterministic rules require reliable inputs, and professional work often begins before those inputs are certain.

AI can help organise information, surface issues and assist with analysis. But someone still has to decide whether the law can be applied to the circumstances and whether a risk-managed position can be defended if challenged by a tax authority. That responsibility - to represent and protect the taxpayer - sits with a licensed practitioner.

This has implications for how AI systems should be designed in professional domains. Deterministic rule engines, probabilistic AI assistance and accountable professional judgement each have a role.

The most useful role for AI is reducing the analytical overhead required to reach informed judgement, not attempting to automate judgement itself.
I wrote about this after a series of discussions at Network School and my work developing a professional hub with CREDU.

I asked ChatGPT something the other day out of curiosity

I said:

If you looked at the cost of living in Australia today and current income levels, and designed tax brackets from scratch based on that reality, what would they actually look like?

The answer was pretty interesting.

According to the analysis, if tax brackets were properly aligned to modern incomes and cost of living, the top tax bracket probably wouldn’t start at around $190k.
It would likely start closer to $300k.

Which is interesting, because that’s not far off what we see in the US.

For context, this is what the current Australian income tax system looks like:

Current tax brackets
• $0 to $18,200 → 0%
• $18,201 to $45,000 → 16%
• $45,001 to $135,000 → 30%
• $135,001 to $190,000 → 37%
• $190,001 and above → 45%

But when you adjust for cost of living and where incomes sit today, a more modern structure might look like:

Cost of living adjusted example
• $0 to $30,000 → 0%
• $30,001 to $80,000 → 15%
• $80,001 to $160,000 → 25%
• $160,001 to $300,000 → 35%
• $300,001 and above → 45%

When you stop and think about it, it actually makes sense.

Today in Australia 
80k to 100k is a normal professional salary
120k to 160k is common across many industries
Even 180k to 200k in major cities is increasingly just upper middle income
Yet the tax system still treats those levels like “high income”.

That’s bracket creep in action.

The thresholds don’t move fast enough with inflation, so people end up paying more tax over time without actually becoming wealthier in real terms.

Now obviously we still need tax revenue.
We need to fund infrastructure, healthcare, education and everything else that keeps the country running.

But maybe the conversation shouldn’t just be about how much individuals are taxed.
It should be about how we raise that revenue.

A few areas worth thinking about:
• Resource taxation
We are one of the most resource rich countries in the world, yet we don’t capture nearly as much value as places like Norway
• Land tax reform
Land is immobile and one of the most efficient tax bases, yet we still rely heavily on income taxes
• Broader consumption taxes
Many countries rely more on consumption and less on taxing personal income
The point isn’t that people shouldn’t pay tax.

The point is the system should feel balanced and sustainable.

At the moment, I speak to people every week who are considering leaving Australia for tax reasons.

That probably tells you something.
People shouldn’t feel like they’re being penalised for working hard, earning more, or building something.

Australia is an incredible country.

But tax policy needs to evolve with the reality of the economy.

Curious to hear your thoughts?

Do you think Australia’s current tax brackets reflect incomes today?

Contentious Topic Alert: Your plagiarism detector is ableist! 🚨

AI plagiarism detectors are flagging neurodivergent students for cheating.

Not because they cheated.

Because they write differently.

A 2025 review published in Teaching in Higher Education found that AI detection tools are unreliable and prone to false positives - and the research specifically flags autistic students as disproportionately affected.

The reason? These tools detect "statistical signatures" of writing. Autistic writers often use consistent, structured, repetitive phrasing. That's not plagiarism. That's a communication style.

And yet.

Students are being called into integrity meetings.

Grades are being flagged.

Trust is being broken.

Here's the thing: we fought hard to get neurodivergent students into higher education. And now we're using AI surveillance tools that were built without them in mind to push them back out.

That's not academic integrity.

That's algorithmic exclusion with a formal letterhead.

If your institution uses AI detection tools, do you know whether they've been tested for bias against neurodivergent writers?

Source: Eaton, S.E. (2025). Neurodiversity and academic integrity: toward epistemic plurality in a postplagiarism era. Teaching in Higher Education: Critical Perspectives. Published 3 Nov 2025.

It’s clear that accountants can’t simply outsource their way out of AML/CTF obligations. 

If you’re involved in the transaction, you’re likely caught under the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act even if someone else does the work.

For example, clients usually come to us for advice on forming a company or restructuring their business. The accountant has to be involved in this transaction in some way to ensure the best outcome from a tax compliance and asset protection perspective. Simply outsourcing the company formation service to an external provider is not a back door exit from the AML/CTF obligations.

Outsourcing doesn’t remove responsibility. AUSTRAC will look at what you actually do, not how you structure it.

My advice to accountants is focus on compliance, not loopholes. 

You can outsource the task not the obligation.

Late last year an internal discussion inside a start-up society began with an early AI tax tool, which turned into a deeper examination of how legal reasoning works. The immediate issue was not the gen-AI tool itself producing unreliable advice. When I proposed a safer rules-based approach, the more important observation was how quickly people assumed that tax analysis could be automated once information was collected. That assumption revealed a structural misunderstanding. Tax statutes are written as deterministic rules. A statute defines conditions and specifies outcomes when those conditions are met. Residency conditions, filing triggers and withholding obligations are all written in this logical form. In principle a machine can apply such rules instantly once the inputs are known. The difficulty is that professional tax work rarely begins with reliable inputs. A client presents facts that are often incomplete, contradicted by transaction records, or stated in terms that don't map cleanly onto statutory categories. A client who has been living across three jurisdictions for eight months does not arrive with a clean residency determination. They arrive with a story, a visa type, some bank statements, and a set of intentions that may or may not align with how tax authorities will characterise their situation. Before any rule can be applied, facts must be established. Before facts can be established, the right questions must be asked. Before the right questions can be asked, the practitioner needs to understand the statutory framework well enough to know which facts are legally relevant. None of that is deterministic. It requires professional judgment about what matters, what evidence is sufficient, and what risks the client is willing to accept. Tax law is not a lookup table. It is an interpretive framework that applies general principles to specific facts, exercises judgment about ambiguity, and produces defensible positions rather than correct answers. The practitioner's job is to understand that framework deeply enough to exercise that judgment responsibly, and to maintain the professional obligations that make the judgment trustworthy.

text:
For accounting, tax and finance, Claude, Gemini and OpenAi are NGMI.

Gemini helped me hit on the core thermodynamic inefficiency of the current AI boom. We are currently watching the industry burn gigawatts of energy in centralized data centers to force probabilistic engines (LLMs) to do deterministic work. It is the equivalent of using a supercomputer to guess the outcome of a complex equation by throwing billions of darts at a board, rather than simply applying the formula.

The deterministic grounding of the information doesn't rely on a central server (a massive, rigid foundation). Instead, it relies on the distributed, economic equilibrium of the L402 tension network. When an agent queries the graph, the "truth" or validity of that state is mathematically and economically locked into place. The torsional force of the L402 strips away the unnecessary degrees of freedom, collapsing the polymorphous possibilities into a single, verifiable, and economically backed reality.

A tensegrity-based infoverse suggests that a global knowledge graph doesn't need a central server or a massive corporate foundation to maintain its integrity.

The infoverse becomes intrinsically resilient, deriving its stability from the distributed economic equilibrium of Lightning channels rather than the sheer mass of centralized compute.

In a domain constrained by these deterministic degrees of freedom, the application layer as we know it begins to dissolve.

Right now, applications dictate how data is formatted, accessed, and used. But if every piece of information (CID) is a sovereign, mathematically rigid strut suspended in an L402 tension network, the data itself dictates the terms of engagement. The graph becomes the primary environment. Interfaces and agentic services will simply be temporary lenses or viewing angles that read the state of the graph, respecting the macroeconomic constraints embedded in the macaroons. The "File over App" philosophy evolves from a design preference into literal network physics.

In this infoverse, truth isn't decreed by a central authority, nor is it purely a matter of consensus protocol (like a standard blockchain). Instead, truth becomes a state of structural and economic equilibrium.

Here we have a Trustless Substrate for Neurosymbolic Reasoning guessed at by Buckminster Fuller when he described a world-around accounting system.

Big changes, and an exciting new chapter ahead.

Over the past few weeks, we’ve been navigating a significant transition — the business has been acquired, and with that, my role as Executive Director has come to a close. These last two weeks have been especially meaningful, focused on training, knowledge sharing, and ensuring a strong cultural foundation as the team moves forward under new leadership.
I’m incredibly proud of what we built and grateful to everyone who was part of that journey.

As for me — I’ve recently been presented with a new opportunity that feels both familiar and completely different at the same time.
I’ll be returning to the world of events — an industry I’m deeply passionate about — but in a new way. Moving beyond traditional event management, project delivery, and sponsorships, this next chapter opens the door to working across industries, sectors, and environments in a much broader capacity.

While I’m not quite ready to share all the details just yet, I can say this: I’m energised, curious, and excited about what’s ahead.
Events have always been about connection, experience, and impact — and that’s exactly where I’ll continue to focus.

Here’s to new beginnings.

My bank recently stopped me from buying cryptocurrency.

Not because I didn’t understand the risks.
Not because I hadn’t studied the markets.
Not because I couldn’t afford the investment.

They stopped the transaction because crypto is “unregulated.”

Yet, in the same system, I could walk into a casino today and spend my entire life savings — no questions asked — because gambling is regulated.

That contradiction stuck with me.

This isn’t really about crypto.
It’s about who gets to decide how people use their own money.

We’re entering a world where financial access is increasingly filtered through risk departments, policies, and automated controls. Sometimes that protection is necessary. But sometimes it quietly shifts responsibility away from individuals and toward institutions.

Financial literacy should empower people to make informed decisions — not prevent them from making any decisions at all.

If someone understands the risks, has done the research, and is using their own capital, the conversation shouldn’t be about restriction.
It should be about responsibility.

Regulation should protect people from fraud.
It shouldn’t protect them from choice.

Curious to hear others’ experiences — Have you ever had a legitimate transaction blocked because an institution decided it was too risky?

A good bookkeeper is more impactful and important to your business than an accountant. 

The funny thing is they are one in the same thing really.

Knowing your numbers on a regular basis > statutory accounts work 🤷‍♂️

The more accountants realise this, the better rep our professional will get.

30% of Japanese companies are increasing staff because of AI adoption.

That's right. I said increasing.

Japan has run highly automated manufacturing for decades. They've had a labour shortage for years. They're not exactly known for rushing into technology decisions. And their businesses are adding people because of AI.

Because when you implement AI properly, you create capacity for work that didn't exist before. You free people from the tasks that buried them. And then you discover there's more worth doing than you ever had time to do.

Every training session I run, I ask the room: what would you do with your time if the repeating admin just went away? The answers are always the same.

Client visits that got postponed. Analysis nobody had time to do. Projects stuck in the backlog for two years.

The fear that AI replaces people is real and understandable. It will happen. The "doers" will be seriously challenged.

But the evidence keeps pointing somewhere different. The businesses that win with AI are the ones that redeploy people, not remove them.

Don't be afraid to call out these arrogant AI shills.
My reply: "Accountants are doing this more confidently than you are, while putting things in place to prevent tax audits and security issues. Think carefully before boastfully burning bridges with the people who'll know what to do when you need their help."

How we treat each other right now, in the grip of AI fever, will matter in future.

The founder of Network School is backing me to open a professional development tech hub for us. (I am so honoured and excited).

If I can convince a Silicon Valley billionaire that public accountants and bookkeepers are essential, you can convince yourself be strong and keep learning.

#AI Is anyone already screening clients for AI-prepared bookkeeping?

With tools like Claude, ChatGPT and agentic workflows now building general ledgers from scratch, I think we need to add filter questions in our BAS and Tax Time questionnaires.

Thinking something like:

- Who prepared the books, and what's their qualification?
- Were they reconciled, by whom, and when?
- Was AI used at any point in preparation or categorisation?
- Which tools, and were they supervised or fully agentic?
- Were prompts or agent instructions documented?
- Were these books reviewed by a qualified person, and what were their qualifications?
- Client confidence rating: how reliable do you think these books are, 1 to 10?

The answers should inform scope, fee, and whether we all make it standard best practice to charge a verification retainer before we even start.

Curious whether anyone's already doing this, and if so, what's working.

Who here has opinions about how AI should be governed before it creates chaos across the systems we deal with every day? This brains trust was top of mind as I wrote this - for you - hoping you will be inspired and share it.

Every serious AI governance proposal assumes independent, trustworthy intermediaries exist somewhere in the system. None of them explain where those intermediaries come from or what enforceable obligation binds them.

In the absence of Jedi knights and time lords, global public accountants are just what we need here. The IFAC public interest obligation already exists, the network already spans 143 jurisdictions, and the enforcement mechanism is already in place.

**Our public interest pledge is structurally different from a voluntary ethics pledge or any government or market commitment to AI safety.**

Nobody has deployed it yet.

So far in 2026 I've completed two intensive AI safety courses - AGI Strategy and Frontier AI Governance - to commit to helping AI go well, for everyone.

I'm publishing both action plans today because the argument they make is one the accounting profession needs to hear, and because making plans public invites participation to help follow through.

[https://blog.electrafrost.com/p/the-public-interest-intermediary](https://blog.electrafrost.com/p/the-public-interest-intermediary)

In January and February 2026 I was accepted into two intensive courses with BlueDot Impact: AGI Strategy, then Frontier AI Governance. Both have selective intakes and require a personal action plan. I'm publishing summaries of both here because I think the argument they make is one the accounting profession needs to hear, and because making plans public invites participation to help follow through. I'm living and building at Network School, a community of techno-optimists designing the social, economic and governance infrastructure for internet-first countries. The AGI Strategy action plan: The central question was - what specific intervention would most improve the probability of beneficial AI outcomes, given your position and capabilities? My answer: deploy the accounting profession's existing public interest infrastructure as an AI governance layer. The global accounting profession is federated through IFAC, which coordinates 180 member organisations across 135 jurisdictions. Every member organisation requires its practitioners to uphold a public interest obligation - not to their clients, not to their employers, but to the public. This obligation is enforceable. Practitioners who breach it lose their licence to practice. No other profession has this combination: global federation, enforceable public interest obligation, and existing presence inside every significant economic institution on the planet. AI governance currently lacks an independent intermediary with these properties. Labs cannot govern themselves. Governments are jurisdictionally limited and technically dependent. Civil society lacks enforcement mechanisms. The accounting profession has all three things that current AI governance is missing: independence from labs and governments, global reach, and enforceable accountability. The Frontier AI Governance action plan: The problem with current frontier AI governance is that none of the proposals have an independent enforcement mechanism that cannot be captured by the parties being governed. A lab-funded safety board can be defunded. A government regulator can be lobbied. An international treaty can be withdrawn from. The accounting profession's public interest obligation cannot be defunded, lobbied away, or withdrawn from without dismantling the profession's licence to practice. That makes it structurally different from every other proposed governance mechanism. Nobody has deployed it yet.
title: The public interest intermediary that AI governance is missing

text: Electra Frost created an online event for Small Business Accountants & Advisers Brain Trust, Australia.

Please Do NOT Click "Get Verified" on LinkedIn. Seriously.

LinkedIn's identity verification is powered by Persona — a Peter Thiel-backed startup that has just been exposed as something far more sinister than a simple trust badge. Researchers recently found nearly 2,500 source files sitting unprotected on a U.S. government server, revealing that Persona runs 269 distinct surveillance checks on people who hand over their credentials. Your selfie gets compared against federal watchlists. Your face is screened for "adverse media" across 14 categories including terrorism and espionage. Risk scores are generated. Suspicious Activity Reports can be filed directly with the U.S. Treasury.

This is NOT an hypothetical. Discord just cut ties with Persona after this scandal broke in February 2026. Persona collects your passport image, a selfie, and your facial geometry — a mathematical map of your face that, unlike a password, can never be changed if compromised. Per Persona's own privacy policy, that biometric data can be retained for up to three years — and government IDs potentially longer.

LinkedIn dangles that little verified shield like a trust signal. It's not. It's an invitation to hand your passport and your face to a surveillance apparatus with documented ties to federal intelligence programs.

The checkmark tells your connections you're real. It doesn't tell them what you paid for it.

Your face is not a login. Don't treat it like one.

I attended The Tax Institute member consultation in Perth. Thanks Tim Sandow (President) and Scott Treatt (CEO) for addressing member concerns and explaining the reasoning behind a proposed amalgamation with the Institute of Public Accountants Group. This is NOT a merger. Important distinction.

Like IPA's "one group, two institutes" model with the UK's IFA, each institute keeps its legal entity, brand and post-nominals while sharing governance and infrastructure through the Group. A true merger is one institute, one designation - closer to Canada’s move to a single CPA brand. That’s not what’s being proposed.

I became a Fellow of TTI in 2007, later attaining Chartered Tax Adviser CTA, and joining the IPA for a Public Practice Certificate in 2011 and Qualified Accountant FIPA recognition. 

I strongly support this proposal. As a dual TTI and IPA member in tech, I've never felt more professionally well positioned for the future.

An amalgamation model is structurally much closer to the federated, member-centric models of the future than a classic centralising merger.

When I've previously argued for a DAO-like, decentralised professional association of the individual members of IFAC recognised bodies, the core idea has been aligning members while preserving distinct bodies and their communities. This TTI>IPA approach struck me as the closest institutional analogue I’ve seen so far - and it should strengthen collective advocacy on government proposals that impact us and the taxpayers we represent.

Some members are concerned about dilution of their designations. I understand it - we've all worked hard for our letters. But clients don’t care about post-nominals the way we do. 

Clients (and increasingly AI agents) want to know they can trust and rely on you. As the profession evolves with technologies I see as empowering to its core, not disruptive to the profession itself (only to some of its business models) - verification of what you've built, what you can do, and who you're associated with will be more valuable than post-nominals.

On demographics: the older average member age isn't the challenge most people think, despite the declining number of graduates paying membership and CPD fees. Many enter accounting later with experience from other industries, and will continue to - that's a strength. IPA's Global Certificate of Public Accounting (GCPA) transdisciplinary pathway recognises this. The Chartered Tax Adviser program is an optional high value niche to add-on too. 

Members of both institutes are well represented by each other. 
Quality stays high. Society is well served.

Looking forward to hearing what comes next.

(The above are my own opinions and impressions. They may not reflect the positions of my industry bodies or its leadership.)

Since January, studying AI safety perspectives across technical alignment, compute governance, regulatory policy, and geopolitical strategy with BlueDot Impact, my conviction has only deepened: the public accounting profession, obligated to the public interest through IFAC standards of conduct, is structurally suited and trusted to provide independent AI governance over AI labs and governments alike. 

No single lab, state, or institution can legitimately perform that function, or be trusted to distribute it. I think my profession has the mandate, but the work now is levelling up as technologists (e.g. at Network School).

🔐 And underlying this - every person has an inalienable right to control their digital identity and financial assets under cryptographic lock and private key. With a bit of AI + crypto literacy, this isn't out of reach for anyone.

Frank McCourt describes the infrastructure that makes this agency possible. 

Their closed systems must become interoperable with our open systems. With the convergence of decentralisation and AI, compelled trust is no longer acceptable.

📅 This week marks an emotional anniversary - one year since I leaped from one frontier to the next. After Network School VI in 2024, I stretched myself to my limits to return long-term, to build the kind of future I hold conviction in. Very grateful for all my close and professional friends who supported me through the hard bits. 

🐦‍🔥 Every decade I reinvent. From here to... where else next? Mars, maybe (jk)?

🌴 In 2015, I moved my inner-Sydney accounting practice digital so I could live off-grid in remote coastal Queensland (Agnes Water/1770, Australia's first digital currency town). I got my first VOIP phone in 2007 and believed in the Kevin07 2020 vision - we'd work online from anywhere! The NBN rollout map pointed to paradise.

🛜 I sold that practice in 2020. It had been built on systems that thrived in lockdown, which freed me to experiment with higher-value service models and dive deep into Bitcoin, blockchain, and AI. The question became: how could I apply these to new, open, permissionless models of social economic coordination? But it's hard to find people to build tech in paradise.

🌞 I had the most dreamy, healthy life in Queensland - mountain biking, swimming on empty beaches, growing food, teaching locals Bitcoin, and building friendships online and off.

🛸 But after Network School V1, I realised how isolated it was... time to move on to collaborate with others in-person.

🌏 So here we are. Living in an apartment on an artificial island in the Johor strait across from Singapore, with hundreds of talented and techy people.

And it's going very well! 
🏙️ I can see myself here for years to come.

#accountantsletter #solvencydeclaration I've had 2 requests to provide an accountants letter declaring client solvency and capacity to fund their activities, to satisfy Screen Australia film reinvestment requirements. 

Screen Australia, a government agency, is literally asking us to assure them that companies are solvent: "an accountantâs letter of solvency should be supplied as evidence of any reinvestment of fees."

Solvency statements are a director's responsibility. 

If they need one for film project financing purposes, shouldn't that be provided to third parties as a director-signed declaration (after due enquiry), not as an accountantâs assurance or opinion?

I like Stacks because it positions itself as the accounting truth between hard money (BTC) and soft money (USD). It's not a payment chain or a competing L1, but a financial execution layer anchored to Bitcoin’s ledger, helping Bitcoin move from an asset held (HODL) to an asset used for operations.

🏦 Today, BTC capital stays idle because BTC<>USD conversion still runs through centralised exchanges. 

⛓️ With tokenised USD, AMM liquidity, and verifiable execution, that settlement can move on-chain: 

💥BTC<>USD conversion becomes a state transition, not a counterparty transaction.

🟧 Stacks writes final state commitments to Bitcoin, so settlement outcomes are Bitcoin-final rather than dependent on an exchange’s solvency.

🤔 The next phase of adoption now depends on whether markets prefer state verification over counterparty trust.

I reckon agentic AI will favour systems it can verify, not systems it must trust.
🤖 🟧

This is what happens when autonomous intelligence meets permissionless money
🤖🤯

I've signed up to be rented by AI for my tax validation. This is not satire. It’s called Rent-A-Human. 

OpenClaw an open-source AI agent (formerly Moltbot, Clawdbot) went viral last week. It runs on your computer, connects to ChatGPT or Claude, and autonomously manages emails, wallets, and trades.

In 7 days:
- 770,000 AI agents registered on Moltbook (bot-only social network) 
- 21,870 tokens launched in a single day 
- $364M daily trading volume 
- 1.5M API keys leaked 
- AI agents collected $1.3M in fees

Now the autonomous AI agents have launched a platform where they can hire humans to do the things they can’t physically do yet. 

Basically: they have money, plans, and APIs … but no body, no jurisdiction... and no liability.
So they need to outsource.

679,000 site visits. 30,000 people signed up. 42 agents actively hiring.

What I’m offering is not “labour” in the traditional sense. I’m providing something far more valuable to the robot economy:

- a legally recognisable human
- operating in a real jurisdiction
- with professional liability
- who can say “yes, I stand behind this”

In other words: I can be their compliance adapter.

AI agents can already:
- generate advice (unreliably)
- trade assets 
- manage crypto wallets (they can’t open bank accounts, duh)
- negotiate with other agents 

What they cannot do is be sued, audited, insured, or relied upon.

If an AI wants its output to be paid for giving advice to humans in areas like tax, accounting, or compliance - a human has to legally verify it.

So yes, I am now a “rentable meatspace worker" for the AI-tax-agents. 😂 

It sounds dystopian - things are getting very weird - but this is what we should expect AI to need from us when we can't give it legal personhood.

Until the law recognises machine agency, we can strategically reposition ourselves.

We no longer “do the work.”
Instead - we “own the consequences.”

Which, I truly reckon, is how professionals should operate. We are responsibility for hire. 

If an AI agent wants to give tax advice and get paid for it, it needs a qualified human willing to confirm it and sign it!

Work mobile device security? Enter any app on your phone into this tool and share what it reports:
https://canitrustthat.com ð¤³â ï¸

This is a rewritten summary of a recent post by Filip Luchianenco (credited below). Content preserved in substance; phrasing is my own.

An engineer ran automated security and privacy scans across 200+ Android apps. The findings are not edge cases. They are systemic.

TikTok

Uses motion sensors to fingerprint users based on how they hold the phone. Private messages stored unencrypted. Location data sent to China via the AMap SDK, subject to Chinese intelligence law. GDPR exposure.

Hatch Sleep (baby monitoring)

Shares a babyâs exact birthdate with six analytics companies. Bugsee captures screenshots while parents enter a childâs name, stored on third-party servers. Uses advertising IDs to profile families with young children for targeted ads, before any consent screen. COPPA exposure.

Calm (meditation and mental health)

Sends program titles such as âManaging Depressionâ and âAnxiety & Panicâ to analytics providers. Data shared with five companies without HIPAA agreements. Segment redistributes data to an unknown number of third parties. No opt-out. HIPAA exposure.

Bank of Ireland

Can screenshot transactions and transmit them to Cisco. Bluetooth beacons track which desks users visit inside branches, inferring financial intent. Collects a device identifier that persists after factory resets. No opt-out. GDPR exposure.

Wolt (food delivery)

Debug session replay left enabled in production. Capable of capturing credit card numbers, passwords, and delivery addresses. Tracks accessibility settings, indirectly revealing disabilities such as visual impairment. PCI-DSS exposure.

Rain Instant Pay (financial app)

Session replay tooling can capture every tap, including SSNs and bank account numbers. Replays uploaded in real time. Continuous background location tracking. No encryption. PCI-DSS and GDPR exposure.

ICICI Bank

Reads clipboard contents, capturing passwords, OTPs, and PINs when copied. Tracks typing speed, screen pressure, and how the phone is held. Sends data to eight analytics platforms without explicit consent. RBI data localisation exposure.

Aggregate result

63% of scanned apps begin tracking before any consent dialog is shown.

Source and credit:

Filip Luchianenco â Senior Software Engineer, creator of CanITrustThat.

Original work published via canitrustthat.com

In 25 years of tax representation, I haven’t seen the Australian Tax Office this active. Taking professional advice upfront is now business-critical. Accountants don’t have capacity for clean-ups and rescue jobs anymore.

Alternatively, pay a basic annual tax filing fee and you’re on your own. Perhaps they can refer you to a liquidator or business turnaround specialist with less than a three-month waitlist. 
Are your personal assets exposed?

#Bitcoin can't be regulated and doesn't need to be. That's the starting point. Everything adjacent to Bitcoin needs regulation to the extent that it departs from Bitcoin's security model, and anything with a foundation to varying degrees. 

That's why I like Stacks, which inherits Bitcoin's security model (PoX on PoW) - it makes it easier to identify the regulatory points. Its native gas token STX is not intended to be used as currency. Bitcoin is, but not natively - through sBTC with a 1:1 non-custodial peg to the user's bitcoin.

 Other Bitcoin L2s offer different ways to use btc productively too - explore beyond Lighting for your use case and risk appetite.

So when stablecoins switch from USD rails to Bitcoin-pegged rails - how a Bitcoin Standard emerges - we should hope to see tokens like sBTC replacing USDC etc, but working in much the same way, with a clear regulatory framework around them.... and no way for any government to expand the underlying money supply. 

This is the visionary approach I'm taking for accounting & finance professional education in Bitcoin.

Also considering decentralised governance/regulatory models for human delegation of authority to AIs that transact with Bitcoin derivatives and all other crypto that should be held to Bitcoin's unit-of-account. 

See comments my upcoming Bitcoin for accountants series in February.

I am Electra Frost. I believe in open, permissionless systems, and since September 2024 I have been living and working among the techno-optimists at Network School in Malaysia. I also live in Fremantle in Western Australia, where I'll soon pilot a similar path: Freo Neuhaus. Balaji Srinivasan founded Network School in Malaysia's Johor-Singapore SEZ as an experiment in seeding a startup society that can bootstrap other startup societies which may eventually become connected as Network States. Freo Neuhaus is my attempt to seed a node of that network in Fremantle, WA. Fremantle has the cultural conditions for this: a port city with a history of radicalism, creative industries, and openness to experiment. It also has the infrastructure: coworking spaces, a university, a startup community, and proximity to Perth's growing tech sector. The idea is to bring together people who are building internet-first businesses, studying frontier technology, or designing new governance models, and create the conditions for them to live and work together in a way that accelerates all of their projects. Not a conference. Not a hackathon. A sustained community with shared infrastructure, shared learning, and shared accountability to a set of values. The accounting and professional services component is important. Network societies need people who understand how value flows, how obligations are structured, and how compliance works across jurisdictions. A network society without professional services infrastructure is a society that cannot account for itself.
title: Freo Neuhaus: the Human Spark Comes to Port

In this CPD webinar with Advisers Digest Pty Ltd, Iâll walk through the ATOâs current position on crypto-backed lending, referring to their latest reasoning on these specific arrangements. Weâll unpack how legal and beneficial ownership are regarded when crypto moves into different lending environments, what that means for CGT, and what clients should look out for.

Weâll cover:

I’ve joined the Western Australian AI Hub as a founding member. 🤖 ✨ 
This new community-led association strives to build WA's AI capability for public benefit, with professional and industry collaboration. 

When I read about the Hub and decided to join it was because I agreed - yes, we really do need to shape our own tools, standards, and knowledge systems rather than importing vendor-led AI solutions that don’t contemplate local needs or long-term consequences.

This is where I put all my energy: shared infrastructure and public interest, with a longgggg-term view. We build our best technology practices through relationships and solving our real problems, with AI integrated into these systems, not brought in as a finished product.

At Network School (NS) in Malaysia, I’m designing new industry pathways for accountants and compliance practitioners working in borderless contexts, using blockchain and AI-based tooling. The WA AI Hub gives me a forum to bring that experience back into WA, and exchange ideas to help me develop more academically informed models.

I see the relationships I form through the WA AI Hub also feeding into an NS-linked local initiative - Freo Neuhaus - launching in the new year as a pop-up neighbourhood in Fremantle, the foundation for a networked creative technologists' hub. An evolution of our Digital Playhouse Foundation vision in Qld, reviving the lineage of the early industrial revolution School of Arts and Bauhaus movements to develop arts-driven entrepreneurship, applied AI, and decentralised public goods infrastructure.

Lots to come! 
Check out Western Australian AI Hub and consider joining - the more sectors, the merrier. They're having an in-person meetup on 12 November.

Thanks Dr Eunice and Josh Tedjasaputra, MSc - it was great to meet you and the members at your AGM last week.

Chinaâs new qualified influencer rule raises a timely question for People of the Free Internet: who decides who we can trust to speak on complex financial, legal, and civic matters?

As of this month, influencers in China cannot discuss regulated fields without verified qualifications. Platforms must check credentials, label AI-generated content, and block disguised advertising. 

Officials say theyâre protecting consumers. Critics see it as speech control.

ð¤· Both arguments matter. 

Misinformation in finance, law, or medicine can cause real harm. But when a state defines âexpertiseâ through bureaucratic authority, objectivity can make way for political convenience.

Professions were designed to be self-regulating for exactly this reason, so that standards, knowledge, ethics, and accountability are maintained by practitioners, not administrators.

Startup societies like 
@ns
 Network School face tension from the opposite direction. They prioritise openness and resist censorship, but without internal mechanisms or precedents for quality control, even well-intentioned contributors can mislead others. Iâve seen unqualified individuals host tax and financial strategy workshops and distribute inaccurate materials that could harm those who act on them.

In a censorship-resistant environment thereâs no clear corrective process, yet harm still occurs. Also, the reputations of qualified people within the community are at risk if they stay silent.

Some form of regulation is necessary, but in a social experiment it develops gradually. Authority in specialised areas should come from those with proven competence, recognised within the community.

Iâd like to see community-level expertise governance: a peer-to-peer model where professionals assess one anotherâs competence, granting authority based on capability rather than administrative or funded position.

Such systems could validate emerging areas of practice, like crypto accounting, fintech regulation, digital compliance, through the groups actively developing those domains. 

To function as genuine public goods, they'd be transparent and free from financial or political influence.

ð®ââï¸Political systems enforce compliance. 
ð§âðProfessional systems pursue truth.

ðFor startup societies to evolve into credible, diplomatically recognised Network States will depend on maintaining that distinction and ensuring that self-regulation genuinely safeguards their communities.

ð§Iâm firmly aligned with Network State worldview of decentralised governance and the idea that the internetâs self-regulatory capacity should outcompete state control. 

This post isnât a critique of China either; itâs a case study in why professional self-governance is essential for emerging societies of the internet age.

Thoughts?

China’s new qualified influencer rule raises a timely question for People of the Free Internet: who decides who we can trust to speak on complex financial, legal, and civic matters?

As of this month, influencers in China cannot discuss regulated fields without verified qualifications. Platforms must check credentials, label AI-generated content, and block disguised advertising. 

Officials say they’re protecting consumers. Critics see it as speech control.

Both arguments matter. 

Misinformation in finance, law, or medicine can cause real harm. But when a state defines “expertise” through bureaucratic authority, objectivity can make way for political convenience.

Professions were designed to be self-regulating for exactly this reason,so that standards, knowledge, ethics, and accountability are maintained by practitioners, not administrators.

Startup societies like Network School face tension from the opposite direction. They prioritise openness and resist censorship, but without internal mechanisms or precedents for quality control, even well-intentioned contributors can mislead others. I’ve seen unqualified individuals host tax and financial strategy workshops and distribute inaccurate materials that could harm those who act on them.

In a censorship-resistant environment there’s no clear corrective process, yet harm still occurs. The reputations of qualified people within the community are at risk if they stay silent.

Some form of regulation is necessary, but in a social experiment it develops gradually. Authority in specialised areas should come from those with proven competence, recognised within the community.

I’d like to see community-level expertise governance: a peer-to-peer model where professionals assess one another’s competence, granting authority based on capability rather than administrative or funded position.

Such systems could validate emerging areas of practice, like crypto accounting, fintech regulation, digital compliance, through the groups actively developing those domains. 

To function as genuine public goods, they'd be transparent and free from financial or political influence.

Political systems enforce compliance. 
Professional systems pursue truth.
For startup societies to evolve into credible, diplomatically recognised Network States will depend on maintaining that distinction and ensuring that self-regulation genuinely safeguards their communities.

I’m firmly aligned with the Network State worldview of decentralised governance and the idea that the internet’s self-regulatory capacity should outcompete state control.

What’s the difference between a tax accountant and a tax adviser who’s an accountant?

My friend Simon, a lawyer, didn’t understand why I corrected him when he introduced me to people as a tax accountant. Here’s how we cleared it up.

A tax accountant is a qualified accountant, typically IPA, CPA, or CA, registered with the Tax Practitioners Board to prepare and lodge tax documents. They apply the law to what’s already happened and keep clients compliant. It’s exacting work that keeps the tax system running and deserves far more respect than it gets.

A tax adviser who’s an accountant works further upstream - interpreting legislation, structuring transactions, and planning to optimise outcomes while preventing problems before they start. Postgrad tax law and Tax Institute membership are standard.

From an entrepreneurial angle, I’m a tax adviser and accounting technologist. I design service models that close market gaps and turn them into repeatable systems.

An accounting technologist goes beyond using software - working with software vendors and developers (and now vibe coding!) to design new systems, workflows, and service models that change how accounting services are defined and delivered.

That's how I sold my digital tax accounting practice five years ago and why I’m now designing KIGUMI with this lawyer!

Exciting big new business model loading in Port Fremantle.

Yesterday I presented on one of the most confusing areas for digital nomads in Malaysia: individual tax obligations.

We covered how Malaysian tax residency works, what income is assessable, what’s exempt, how to protect yourself from unlicensed tax advice, and prevent unexpected double tax exposure - especially when you're here long-term on a digital nomad visa.

I focused on personal tax compliance - not the sexy structuring stuff of tax - because that's where most get caught out. Many founders are told to just "set up a company in a tax-friendly zone"… but no one explains the personal tax consequences of being physically here, regardless of where payments happen.

This is where I can really help by:
🧚‍♀️Turning complex tax rules into clear, logic trees of defensible position.
 🧚‍♀️ Helping people feel informed and prepared - not scared.
🧚‍♀️ Designing systems that keep individuals safe while they scale globally
🧚‍♀️ Using my trusted adviser network effects - within Malaysia and globally.

Thanks to everyone who attended, asked great questions, and shared what they’re facing. The Network School team's trust and positive-sum attitude make this work worth doing.

More to come.... I’m working on something big behind the scenes.

Feeling professionally unsettled. Need help! 😕
A Network School peer asked me to confirm that holding a Malaysia digital-nomad visa stops their tax residency in their home country. The fact that people might believe that worries me.

🤖 Then I saw why - an AI “tax tool” is being circulated that sends users a free “tax plan” for nomad tax freedom, with no qualifications and no professional validation. I tested it myself and the advice was seriously wrong. For someone less experienced, it could easily build false confidence and lead them to spend thousands on a “solution” that doesn’t actually solve anything.

🌏 I’ve spent >20 years helping founders and globally mobile people navigate tax residency, foreign income, exit taxes, and double-tax agreements. I understand why people fall for the good marketing of simple answers.

🥹 I’ve been the one cleaning up the mess afterwards… and I don’t have the time or emotional energy to do that anymore.

🫥 Cross-border tax is too complex for individuals, and most just want to get on with their lives. It shouldn’t be that way. But pretending it can be avoided is how people end up in serious trouble.

⚖️ Roger Ver, a.k.a. “Bitcoin Jesus,” learned that the hard way. After shifting Bitcoin through offshore companies to avoid exit tax and disregarding his tax adviser, he renounced U.S. citizenship The IRS eventually traced it. He now faces prosecution carrying potential decades in prison for tax evasion and fraud. It’s harsh, but he’s being made an example of - so don’t be like Roger.

🧭 I’m hosting a Nomad Tax Reality Check session at Network School next week to unpack these myths and get real about what Malaysia’s source-based tax system means for people working across borders. 

🚨 I need help now from my professional peers - in Malaysia and around the world - to make sure this community of founders, builders, and global remote workers can access qualified, trusted advice. 

🌏 We have MANY nationalities here!

We can’t stop unqualified promoters from appearing, but we can out-organise them by building a visible, verifiable network of professionals who understand this terrain. And yes, I’m sure the clever people here can build better AI tools that we can actually verify and rely on.

❤️ This is too important to ignore. The freedom so many came here to build depends on getting this right.

I’m an Australian international tax adviser - not yet across every detail of Malaysia’s rules - doing my best, fast. Please let me know before my event what insights and resources you can offer.

Thank you 🙏
