Congratulations to Ernie Painter on 40 years with the Institute of Public Accountants! At the IPA WA Fellows Lunch, I enjoyed chatting with Ernie about AI, and his brilliant career spanning banking, superannuation, accounting, and unions.

Our conversation turned to decentralisation and Bitcoin, as I'm often asked to explain it. Ernie raised concern for the future income of his author clients as Big Tech AI exploits their work without regard for copyright. As an arts accountant for 25 years, I share that concern. I began studying blockchain at RMIT in 2018 after seeing how creative technologists could use it to design models that return value from big-tech silos to creators.

I explained how authors and artists could attach their works to programmable digital assets on blockchains under their direct control, enabling AI agents to license them to generative platforms and stream payments directly to their wallets. 

Crypto is internet-native, programmable money - enabling our AIs to transact with other AIs on our behalf. AI canât use bank accounts. It needs crypto.

Decentralised network models like this remove reliance on collection societies or agents - but they can only succeed with regulatory and tax reform that recognises crypto assets as distinct from traditional property and excludes crypto and DeFi transactions from the CGT regime.

Ernie lit up at the idea of technology serving creators. This is why Iâm at Network School - and working to position public accountants as leaders in shaping these transitions and maximising social impact with Bitcoin and blockchain.

Ernie asked me for a book recommendation. I immediately suggested Joanna Pennâs Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, and Virtual Worlds: The Impact of Converging Technologies on Authors and the Publishing Industry. It clearly explains how authors can harness these tools rather than be displaced by them.

I think that Ernie will keep following where our profession is heading for many years to come.
:)

If you're an accountant still brushing off Bitcoin as irrelevant or speculative, you're missing the most important monetary shift of our time - one that directly impacts the wealth, equity, and financial resilience of the clients we serve.

Accounting, at its core, is about truth in value, ethics, integrity of records, and the protection of purchasing power over time. 

Yet the foundation of our current system - fiat currency masquerading as money - is infinite in supply and debased annually by policy decisions far beyond our control.

This hidden taxation through inflation disproportionately harms wage earners, freelancers, and SMEs - while inflating the asset prices held by the already wealthy.

It is a professional accountantâs duty to understand this imbalance, not excuse it.

Bitcoin matters because it reintroduces objective scarcity â the first since the end of the gold standard. It is not a speculative asset. It is a programmable accounting primitive on an immutable, time-stamped ledger.

Fixed in supply (21 million), yet endlessly divisible, auditable in real-time, resistant to manipulation, and borderless by design â Bitcoin offers a new foundation for trust-minimised finance, long-term value preservation, and even public credentialing infrastructure for our profession.

The Supply Pyramid says it all:

Fiat: Infinite
Gold: Scarce
Bitcoin: Fixed

Which would you trust to store value over decades?
Which supports sustainable, intergenerational wealth planning? 

Come on, now... how many negatively geared investment properties should we be enriching banks through instead of simply stacking sats??

Bitcoin-native financial instruments, crypto accounting systems, micropayment rails, decentralised professional networks, AI authentication, and timestamped attestations are already emerging.

âï¸ The accountants who study this now will be the ones designing the future of our role. 
âï¸ The rest will keep reporting inflation-distorted figures on a ledger that no longer tells the truth. âï¸Which will serve the public interest - and be recognised for it?

This isn't about ideology. It's about relevance.

Bitcoin is no longer fringe. It's the foundation layer of the next monetary era.

We should be the ones documenting it, and framing its relevance to our profession. Now.

#BitcoinIsAccounting
#AccountantsAreBitcoin
#StudyBitcoin
#TalkAboutBitcoin

Bitcoin’s strength is monetary integrity, not day-to-day payments. Forcing it into retail transactions ignores what the market actually wants: dollar stability for spending, Bitcoin for saving.

Stablecoins are already handling the transactional layer - cheap, fast, familiar - and are attached to the current global reserve asset and currencies. They’re not competition; they’re infrastructure. They help onboard users without asking them to take on short term volatility up front.

When Bitcoin becomes the base layer, global reserve asset, stablecoins will have done the bridging. Trying to make BTC the point-of-sale token right now is premature and distracts from the bigger goal: building a sound financial foundation on Bitcoin the network.

BTC-for-payments isn't essential - what matters is that everything settles back to Bitcoin's fixed supply. 

Let's build THAT. 
Who is building that already? 🙂

I'm pleased to have been quoted making this important distinction when advising SMSF trustees: “Bitcoin and crypto are not interchangeable terms from an investor perspective. Bitcoin is the only asset that has emerged from the crypto ecosystem with a credible claim to being a long-term store of value.” 

Bitcoin’s proof-of-work and UTXO-based security model give it enduring integrity - the basis for the trust and long-term value that conservative investors seek. That’s why SMSF trustees should treat Bitcoin differently from everything else in “crypto” when preparing their investment strategies, alongside other considerations like custody and trustee experience.

Article linked in comments below.

Thanks to the Institute of Public Accountants for helping disseminate this important knowledge, and to Paul Holland for your excellent contribution to the piece.

Australian Public Accountants, CPAs, CAs: is it time we step back from "facilitating legal or financial arrangements" that fall into AML/CTF territory? With Tranche 2 reforms coming, I’m re-evaluating services like:
- Entity setup 
- Acting as ASIC agent, registered office, handling correspondence
- Structuring advice or involvement in offshore arrangements
- Assisting with bank or crypto accounts
- Using outsourced formation agents
- Bookkeeping/VCFO work involving software with payment initiation for approval

I’ve appreciated the service providers that have supported our roles in providing ASIC agent functions,  but I’ve never been fully comfortable. This year, I plan to go hands-off and collaborate with a commercial lawyer to look after my clients.

But if we all step back, will we just see more clients trying to set things up themselves without legal advice, which may be cost prohibitive - and us only catching the fallout at tax time?

Curious how others are thinking this through.

Why we are building Network State at Network School ns.com - my key takeaways from Balaji Srinivasan's orientation talk for our 100 new techo-optimist June recruits. ð¤©

Think of a country that starts with internet communities - bringing the cloud to land. Internet-First is like a new political term for a new version of Nationhood. 

It was easier to create Bitcoin than to reform the Fed. And itâs easier to build Network States than to reform broken Nation States. The internet is now the only counterweight to China-scale power. Not adversarial - small nations with a flag but no money are warming to the crypto economy and will welcome NS Nodes.

Think of Network State as a globally distributed country - like a digital archipelago with economic mass.

We arenât building a utopia. Boring tech people are building boring, resilient, economically viable jurisdictions - enterprise-grade SAAS meets jurisdiction-as-a-service. Think startups, DAOs, professionals, developers, and service operators improving on democracy - structuring legitimacy on-chain, with governance primitives and auditable logic.

China can build the most megacities, factories, and warships - but it canât build Bitcoin and freedom tech. It canât build the freewheeling, opt-in logic of the global internet. What China is to scale in the physical world, the internet is to scale in the digital one. The new balance isnât the US vs China. Itâs China vs the Global Internet. 

The US no longer wants to be the centre of the global economy. Business is moving to Asia. But the internet moves faster - itâs already rebalancing global capital and control. Itâs where we build.

Internet First is now a political identity. Itâs more than communications - itâs money (Bitcoin), contract law (blockchain), democratic participation (DAOs), and equal citizenship (peer-to-peer). No visas. No borders. No second-class citizens.

The cloud forms the land. We are building Internet Places: nodes, campuses, jurisdictions, zones. A new internationalism - coded, composable, and aligned to open values.

Network School is where we experiment, co-design and train the builders of these nodes. The builders include accountants, lawyers and engineers of a borderless system. The only thing with the scale to balance Chinaâ¦ is us.

The scale of what we can do on the Internet in 20 years is phenomenal. Just look at the last 20 years and add Bitcoin!

Here in Malaysia/Singapore we are building a startup society to build and bootstrap other startup societies... as nodes, that could all be very different to each other! (cultures, lifestyle etc)

People keep talking about using Bitcoin for payments. This idea is holding us back. 

Jack Dorsey recently said: “I think it has to be payments for [Bitcoin] to be relevant on the everyday.”

I fundamentally disagree.

The next wave of adoption isn’t about buying coffee. It’s about unlocking real utility. Bitcoin is a generational asset. Most holders don’t want to spend it; they want to preserve it. 

So the real question becomes: how do you make Bitcoin useful without spending or selling it?

That’s where Bitcoin DeFi comes in. Today, you can bridge your Bitcoin to Layer 2s and borrow stablecoins against it. No selling. No leaving the Bitcoin ecosystem. Yet, according to Binance, only 0.8% of Bitcoin is being used in DeFi. That’s less than 1% of nearly $1 trillion in onchain capital sitting idle.

As Bitcoin’s block rewards shrink every halving, miner incentives will need new sources of fuel. Scaling to Bitcoin L2s like Stacks is how we get there without compromising the base layer.

If we want Bitcoin to succeed long term, we need to help people do more onchain—lend, borrow, earn, build, not just spend.

The idea of Bitcoin as “digital gold”? It’s already here. Countries are holding it in their strategic reserves. That doesn’t scream “let's buy a latte” to me. It screams, "let's make Bitcoin productive".

I do agree with Jack on one thing: Bitcoin must be relevant in daily life. But relevance doesn’t only come from spending. It comes from functionality. The future of Bitcoin isn’t just hodling or spending it. It’s building real utility and earning on it. Builders who enable real utility for Bitcoiners without compromising its core will shape the next decade.

Let Bitcoin be the store of value it was born to be. Then let the Bitcoins flow 🟧

Full piece on CoinDesk https://t.co/5jJmVUbSE0

hashtag

Alex agreed we need to fight for ownership of culture and our humanness - but said the more urgent problem now is the sudden centralised control happening at the AI foundation model layer, which won’t be fixed by changing internet architecture alone.

My notes from Alex’s talk in our Network School Library today:

Problem:
Frontier AI is being captured by a few big labs.
Training costs are so high, with massive staffing and energy costs, that only the most resourced corporations can participate.
Competitors need $50m just to get started. If you don’t win first, you lose everything. No second play. Shut down. Game over.

“Open source” in AI is a façade. You can’t fork Llama.
Public lock-out on model development. You can’t participate unless you’re inside a core research team.
Meta competes on openness but not capability.
The open vs closed gap is widening fast. 

Our culture is already being shaped by a handful of model owners.
People will learn, decide, and build under models they cannot interrogate.
The effect will be like TikTok ×100 on public thinking.
Deplatforming of businesses and loss of creators' authenticity are inevitable at this scale.
Attempts at regulatory capture have already been made — and will be made again.
Dollar auction dynamics guarantee centralisation will accelerate unless stopped.

Solution:
Decentralised Protocol Learning.
* Distributed training
* Verifiable contribution
* Fractional ownership
* Unextractable weights
* Crypto incentives to sustain decentralised AI

Building the biggest global model economy that is open, permissionless, and sustainable.

Our Biggest Fight warns us all in plain language. Alex’s technical talk today clarified for me how we'll use crypto and decentralised approaches to recapture our future at the AI model layer - and why the threat is bigger and coming faster at us than most realise.

But we have to get this right, with the right incentive model, because real open AI will be unstoppable and there'll be no going back to fix it.

Establishing Bitcoin as a global unit of account is inevitable while central bank monetary policy continues to devalue fiat and we move toward a Bitcoin Standard. With a fixed supply, Bitcoin becomes a neutral, incorruptible measuring stick for all economic output.
📏
At full monetisation, 1 BTC ≈ US$5.5 million (based on 2025 global GDP of ~US$115.5T), making 1 sat ≈ $0.05 - too large for many real-world transactions. Lightning enables millisats (1/1,000 of a sat), but only off-chain. The base layer cannot process sub-sat units. 
⛓️
To support finer denominations with Bitcoin finality - for all means of exchange, not just in BTC or sats - we need anchored, programmable layers like Stacks. These enable BTC-pegged tokens to function as currency, support lending and stable instruments, and preserve the integrity of the base asset. 
🌏
If Bitcoin is to serve as a global unit of account, it requires on-chain layers that extend its utility without compromising its monetary integrity. That’s what platforms like Stacks offer.
📚
Everyone will "get it" when the world's accountants get it. That’s why my focus at Network School is on contextualising the Global Accountants Competencies Framework for a Bitcoin economy. 
🚀 
(special thanks to Institute of Public Accountants for creating an excellent microcredentials framework that I can build on and make open-source!)
🙏

Jack Dorsey's encouragement to use BTC for everyday payments is problematic. He should champion Bitcoin's pivotal function as global Unit of Account, and stressing the importance of Bitcoin L2s like Stacks and its sBTC 1:1 trustless pegged asset approach in achieving that.

Bitcoin is the world's no.1 ledger - not a payments platform - it's the most secure settlement layer for ALL digital currencies and financial instruments on ALL chains to be held to account by, valued, measured, and trusted. 

It's not a mere means of exchange - its far more than currency - it's the most significant monetary technology. 

We're not playing shop with sats anymore! 

By holding any store of value in BTC, we are part owners of the future decentralised world bank ...we'll earn network fees from settlements. That's what "be your own bank" really means.

With that in mind, expecting people to spend sats now is quite frankly deluded. 

Without Bitcoin as our Unit Of Account for all currencies, to eventually flip the USD, every MoE adoption attempt will fail, putting a Bitcoin Standard further out of reach. That's because businesses cannot use BTC as standard for pricing, contracts and financial reporting (e.g. El Salvador).

Why do Bitcoin L2's matter?

“In a few decades when the reward gets too small, the transaction fee will become the main compensation for nodes (miners). I'm sure that in 20 years there will either be very large transaction volume or no volume.”

- Satoshi Nakamoto. bitcointalk. org

Lightning is not the only L2. In the next 5-10 years people will want to make their BTC programmable and collatorise it, not spend it. For "very large transaction volume" look beyond Lightning - the larger Bitcoin L2 ecosystem since Ordinals and AI woke up the Bitcoin builders ... the network fees and latent liquidity of Bitcoin Economy signals!

GovFi: A Decentralized Model for Government Efficiency - crowdsourcing government functions to citizens and rewarding verified contributions, reducing bureaucratic waste, fraud, and inefficiency. Instead of funding bloated public services through excessive taxation and borrowing, this system incentivizes real outcomes through decentralized, transparent processes.

The machine is Pacioli.ai, a decentralized physical infrastructure network 
hashtag
#DePIN built by Auditchain Labs AG. By automating financial and regulatory oversight using AI and blockchain, costly administrative layers are replaced with a distributed validation system. Node operators verify machine-readable disclosures, creating integrity while slashing compliance costs.

When working on CREDU I envisioned a Decentralized Professional Association (DPA) of Accountants forming, reinventing regulations, and claiming a social mandate to take over the functions of tax agencies like the IRS, ATO, and HMRC. 

They told us we'd be the obsolete ones. Wrong. 😉

Instead of taxpayers funding inefficient government agencies, tax and economic governance would be handled peer-to-peer, with verified, on-chain accountability.

Taxation becomes a fraction of the cost, compliance is real-time and accurate, and public financial oversight is restored to qualified people - away from government employees and corporate contractors.

🟧 Big Citizen, Small Government. 

The technology is here. Now, will governments make way for it? They will eventually if we get the public onside and behave competitively. 

Your thoughts on how we can do that?

Last year, I joined Balaji Srinivasan's Network School V1 (Sept–Dec 2024) in Singapore/Malaysia, a social experiment to initiate the emergence of network nations - as cloud communities materialising in the physical world to form startup societies.

After years of isolation living in remote Australia and collaborating online, I thrived in this communal environment - surrounded by high-energy, mission-driven people who inspired and supported my true purpose! 🟧 😎 

We lived, worked, learned, and built on our ideas together - without the usual distractions of running a household. Meals, gym, cleaning, and workspaces were all provided, allowing us to focus on our businesses and projects. 

🏫 Now, Network School is evolving:

🔹 V1 – Startup Society (2024): The inaugural 128 person cohort of 'dark talent' cohort gathered in Malaysia to test this new model of community - combining education, work, and governance in a shared physical space.

🔹 V2 – Society-as-a-Service (2025): This year-long cohort from March 2025 expands to 256 members, forming a permanent, location-flexible community.

🔹 V3 – Society from Scratch (2025+): A permanent Network School campus near Singapore will take shape, designed to house thousands of technologists and innovators.

🔹 V4 – Scaling the School: Prefabricated construction will make replication easy, allowing similar communities to be built worldwide.

🔹 V5 – Start Your Own Society: Members will take these learnings to launch their own startup societies, backed by a global network.

-----------------

✈️ After working here in Perth, I’ll return to Network School V2/V3 - this time, contributing to their plans for building a permanent campus. What might a futurist accounting faculty look like in this new Harvard...? 🤔 

🌏 The idea of scoping a Perth location for V4/V5 also intrigues me. It’s in the same time zone as NS Singapore/Malaysia, making it an easy extension of the network for me as an Australian.

Bitcoin in the Mist: High Altitude Perspective 🍵 🏔️ The most delightful time I had in Malaysia was in the Cameron Highlands, a mountainous plateau where the air was crisp and cool, tea plantations stretched into the clouds, their mist rolling in like something out of a dream.

It’s a funny place - Tudor-style architecture rising from the rainforest, relics of an old British world that persist in the heart of Malaysia.

Exploring winding roads and hidden paths through the tea plantations, disappearing into the mist… you’d climb without knowing what lay ahead, expecting something worthwhile to eventually reveal itself.

Strawberries grow abundantly in the terraced farmlands of the valleys, thriving in this strange highland microclimate. They're made into the most genuinely strawberry-flavoured ice cream in the world - so fresh, so real, the taste still lingers.

Climbing that misty path, barely able to see a metre ahead, reminds me of Bitcoin adoption - the uncertainty, the need for conviction, and the decision to ascend without guarantees.

Bitcoin is not an endpoint; it is a process of discovery. The path forward is uncertain, but the principles remain clear.

Some hesitate at the base, waiting for perfect clarity - regulations, mainstream adoption, the familiar structure of traditional finance. Others have already started climbing, believing that the path is worth following, even if the summit is not yet visible.

You don’t wait for absolute clarity before moving; you move because the direction is right.

We have two choices:
✅ Remain anchored in legacy systems, in the comfort of what has always been.
✅ Step into something new, uncertain, but full of potential.

The fog will clear for everyone eventually, but by then, the best vantage points will already be taken.

Bitcoin rewards conviction. So does life.

Those who seek safety in old systems will remain anchored to the past. Those who embrace uncertainty will build the future.

The best spots at the summit won’t belong to those who waited for the view to be obvious.

They will belong to those who climbed into the unknown when others stood still. 

Some paths only reveal their value after you start walking. 

🤔 ❓ What makes someone take the first step? Thinking on this... 
✍

ð¨ Australia's Financial Reporting Future â Where Are the Digital Assets? ð¨
Treasuryâs consultation on merging the FRC, AASB, and AUASB into a new statutory body sets out priorities including sustainability reporting, public sector financial reporting, audit and assurance standards, and alignment with IFRS. Yet, digital asset financial reporting is completely absent! ð¥¶ 

Ignoring digital assets at this stage is a serious oversight. Other jurisdictions are already integrating digital asset financial reporting into their regulatory frameworks, so accounting professionals are equipped to address the complexities of Bitcoin, tokenised finance, stablecoins, and blockchain-based financial instruments.

If this new statutory body is to position Australiaâs financial reporting for the future, digital asset reporting must be part of its mandate. The merged entity must have a DIGITAL ECONOMY REPORTING STANDARDS COMMITTEE within its governance structure and ensure the technical committee includes expertise in:

ð On-chain financial reporting
ð Proof-of-reserves methodologies
ð Blockchain based audit tools and financial controls
ð Subledger reconciliation for digital assets
ð FASB ASU 2023-08 and IFRS discussions on fair value accounting
ð A deep understanding of the unique characteristics of Bitcoin and DeFi
... and more

Without this, Australia will lack the necessary financial reporting standards for the digital economy, putting accountants at a disadvantage and creating regulatory uncertainty for businesses operating in this space.

The Board's strategic direction and its technical committees should make digital asset reporting a primary focus area of standard-setting in the new body. 

This is a once is a generation opportunity for Australia to establish a modernised financial reporting framework! 

Treasury is seeking submissions - I encourage accountants to engage and push for digital asset reporting to be included.

Go here: https://treasury.gov.au/consultation/c2025-536708

A Bitcoiner in Malacca: Lessons in Misplaced Trust: When I wandered Malacca’s streets, its history struck a chord. In 1511, the Portuguese arrived in Malacca, presenting themselves as allies offering collaboration and mutual benefit. The Malaccans, operating in good faith, welcomed what appeared to be a promising opportunity. 

However, beneath the surface, the Portuguese were strategising a take over. They exploited Malacca’s openness and weaknesses, and conquered them, marking the beginning of its colonisation and delivering a harsh lesson about misplaced trust.

Bitcoin, founded on principles of openness, decentralisation, and independence, is designed to eliminate the need for trust. Yet, like Malacca’s leaders welcoming the Portuguese to a seat at their table, Bitcoiners today are accepting political figures like Trump, attracted by his rhetoric on freedom and innovation.

As history shows, not everyone acts in alignment with their words - what seems like support may instead lead to harm. The lesson here to scrutinise motives and core values before giving anyone a foothold in our communities. 

Malacca’s story, like the Bitcoin community's, reminds me of the importance of protecting what we’ve built from those who see opportunities to exploit rather than strengthen.

Beautiful city - needs at least 2 days to explore properly so I'll head back this year :)

...to be continued

Like a modern-day Tintin, I’m setting off on new adventures as a digital nomad (or slowmad), to chart my path globally and share my journey along the way. Selling my house and all my possessions, reduced to two suitcases. I’ll own everything digitally and I’ll be happy!

Life on remote coastal acreage in Queensland since leaving Sydney in 2016 was an amazing chapter. Living off-grid with mountain biking to deserted beaches, drinking rainwater, growing food, creating Digital Playhouse Foundation with🟧Gordon Christian, mining Bitcoin with solar, and collaborating with Bitcoin and web3 visionaries around the world while running my online accounting practice has been ideal in many ways.

But it hasn’t been without challenges. Pre-COVID, I balanced my country life with an apartment in Brisbane, but since then, isolation crept in. Even with great local friends and travels to events, I felt the loneliness acutely after returning from El Salvador in 2022. I missed the hardcore social purpose, Bitcoin hackathons, and entrepreneurial spirit I found there, and it made me realise how stuck I’d become in my "remote paradise." I stayed, but I stagnated - feeling trapped by my house, possessions, and personal circumstances.

That all shifted in 2024. After meeting up at WAWEB3 in Perth, my friend Andrew Noble suggested we go to the Network State Conference in Singapore, to learn about what's happening with Balaji Srinivasan's vision of decentralised, borderless societies. A few weeks before the event, I applied to join the Network School cohort - Balaji's 90-day startup-society cohort on an island near Singapore - and was accepted!

While living communally with digital nomads and "slowmads" from around the world, I discovered the parallel societies movement. This is far more significant than digital nomadism - they're physically iterating borderless, global communities that I can contribute to. 

Network School changed my perspective of "home" from nationhood to networkhood. I never consented to be defined by the country I was born in. I'm free to align myself with an internet-based society of shared values, ambitions, economy and vision for the future... if I step into it.

Since returning to Australia at Christmas, I’ve been preparing for this migration. Getting rid of all the "stuff" I no longer need, along with lingering self-imposed limitations.

First stop: A few months in Perth for an exciting accounting project and conferences, while waiting for Network School to re-open in Asia. Some pop-up cities in El Salvador and Brazil, with Network School connections, are also calling.

I need to immerse myself in the borderless, Bitcoin shaped future I believe in, and will be sharing my journey - hoping it inspires others to explore new paths too.

(Photo: The TinTin Shop in Singapore. My itinerant parents called me TinTin because I was always exploring, and could be relied on to find my way out of trouble - and back to the hotel.)

Bitcoin's pivotal monetary function as a Unit of Account redefines how we measure, compare, and transact economic value, to create a consistent global standard. Most people understand Bitcoin as a Store of Value or Means of Exchange, but its potential as a Unit of Account deserves greater exploration. A Unit of Account is one of the foundational functions of money, providing a standard for pricing goods, services, and contracts. It enables us to compare values across time, place, and context. When Bitcoin becomes the UoA, all economic activity is priced in Bitcoin or satoshis, creating a single, universally accessible reference point for value. Accountants are the custodians of value measurement. We design the systems that record, report, and interpret economic activity. If Bitcoin becomes the global UoA, accountants are the professionals who will operationalise that transition - designing the accounting frameworks, the reporting standards, and the compliance systems that make it work in practice. The Composite Fractionalized Money approach: Rather than a binary switch from fiat to Bitcoin UoA, I propose a composite approach in which Bitcoin serves as the reference unit while operational currencies remain the transactional medium. This mirrors how gold once functioned as a reference standard while national currencies handled day-to-day transactions. Every balance sheet, every income statement, every contract would carry a Bitcoin-denominated reference value alongside its nominal currency value. Network accountants - practitioners operating in the Network State context, across jurisdictions, with Bitcoin-native clients - are the natural pioneers of this framework.
title: Bitcoin Unit-of-Account: Establishing a Global Standard with Network Accountants

In a multi-chain, multi-currency future, Bitcoin serves as our ideal Unit of Account for consistent value measurement and pioneering a new financial paradigm. ​Most see Bitcoin as a Store of Value (SoV) or Means of Exchange (MoE), but its pivotal function as a Unit of Account (UoA) is less explored. How do we get there? A multi-chain approach with composite fractionalised money may make it work.

A UoA is essential for measuring and comparing economic value, providing a standard for valuing goods, services, and transactions—much like the US dollar in pricing and financial reporting. For global "cloud countries," adopting Bitcoin as UoA represents a logical shift away from reliance on the USD as the global reserve asset.

I'm presenting this session at 
hashtag
#TheNetworkSchool to share research on:

​ 🅱️ Bitcoin’s Function as a Unit of Account: 
The foundational purpose of money; the "Standard of Contract" concept; Bitcoin as a global reserve asset and base layer for settlements; Fixed supply and decentralization make it a unique economic standard.

​ 🌴 Lessons from Próspera: 
The first startup society (in Honduras) to pioneer Bitcoin as a Unit of Account - their bold purpose, novel tax system, challenges with eGovernance and accounting integrations, regulatory hurdles.

​ 💳 The Subscription State: 
Composite Fractionalized Money offers an approach to developing the economic framework for a society-as-a-service model using a Bitcoin Unit of Account, addressing the challenges of globally distributed startup societies.

​ ⛓️ Dynamic Payment System Design: 
A smart contract-powered mechanism for Composite Fractionalized Money, integrating sBTC (trustless BTC peg on Stacks) and stablecoins to mitigate volatility. 

​This approach is aimed at:

​ 1️⃣ Achieving consistent value measurement, flexible payments, and predictable economic outcomes for merchants and participants (e.g., Subscription States and members; e.g. Network Schools globally). 

​ 2️⃣ Facilitating practical implementation of Bitcoin as a Unit of Account, globally.

Commonwealth Bank’s duplicate transaction error exposes the risks of centralised banking, where systemic errors and mismanagement can lead to issues like duplicated payments. A Bitcoin-anchored ledger would prevent duplicates by using decentralised consensus, where each transaction is validated, cryptographically secured, and permanently recorded. 

Backed by proof-of-work, code, and maths, a Bitcoin network layer design would ensure that every unit can only be spent once, as any duplication attempts would be detected and rejected by the networks. 

If our money were pegged to Bitcoin, each transaction would be transparent and require network consensus before confirmation, eliminating accidental duplication and bank fraud, and ensuring the integrity of the entire system.

Now envision our money as a token on a Bitcoin Layer 2 network like sBTC on Stacks, but using a zero-knowledge-first chain for customer privacy. This setup would bring Bitcoin finality to bank settlements, ensuring fast, efficient processing while prioritising both security and privacy. 

Irregularities would be detected and stopped on-chain, blending the reliability of traditional banking with Bitcoin’s superior security model. Understanding how Proof-of-Work (Bitcoin) and Proof-of-Transfer (Stacks) operate reveals how this system could also reduce agency costs tied to trust.

Finance and accounting professionals should be advocating for this shift—using Bitcoin-based Layer 2 solutions would provide a more secure, accountable foundation for financial systems, making duplicated transactions and fraud nearly impossible. Extend this Bitcoin Layer 2 model to nation-state treasuries and reserve banks to completely reshape global finance (a truly great reset). 

Plus, banks would see lower operational costs and higher profits, redistributed to network participants. With Bitcoin, you're not just a customer; you're a network member. And that’s where the real value lies.

How can internet commerce overcome the cognitive barriers associated with micropayments to make them a practical option for consumers?

The article "Micropayments and Mental Transaction Costs" from the Satoshi Nakamoto Institute explores why micropayments haven't caught on yet. 

It argues that the primary barrier to the adoption of micropayments is the mental transaction costs incurred by consumers. These costs arise from the cognitive effort required to manage small transactions, which often outweigh the technological benefits of reduced transaction fees. 

The paper distinguishes between technological transaction costs, which have been minimised by micropayment technologies, and cognitive costs, which remain significant. 

It suggests that efforts to reduce these cognitive costs, such as through automation or improved user interfaces, could make micropayments more viable.

However, the challenge remains in effectively communicating consumer preferences to software, which is crucial for reducing their mental accounting burden. 

Highly technical but very important for Bitcoin Builders to consider. 👷‍♀️

 I know that my CREDU solution, by ditching the subscription cost model for sBTC micropayments, will be vastly more cost effective than other CPD tracking and compliance options for professional accounting bodies and their members. But they'd rather spend more on costly legacy internet commerce subscriptions because it's easier to understand. So I'll need a strategy on this.

🤔

#BitcoinNashville Bitcoin Builders Conference Highlights Pt 2 of 4: All Roads Lead to Bitcoin! See some sessions I flagged for digital accounting thought leaders on how Bitcoin is perceived and used, from its global finance role to practical applications in business. (links in comments).

As more institutions recognise Bitcoin as a legitimate asset and seek to unlock liquidity, the Bitcoin community debates how to balance its original ethos of decentralisation with the development of new functionalities that introduce different trust models.

WHAT IS A BITCOIN LAYER?

This discussion evaluated Bitcoin layers and the trade-offs and trust assumptions involved. Andre Serrano explained sBTC, a method for moving BTC into Bitcoin layers, and how Stacks fits into the Bitcoin L2 landscape. 

Janusz Grzegorz is developing a risk analysis framework, and believes that scaling blockchains with blockchains won't work vs centralised systems that can offer people a right to self-custody and privacy. Andre argues that different decentralised solutions can uphold those rights, that Bitcoin should ossify and to keep experimentation off its base layer.

Bitcoin layers extend BTC functionality with smart contracts and offer cheaper, faster payments. Key questions include custody of funds, sovereignty, verifiability on Bitcoin, and settlement finality. If users don't know the risks, can they recover their BTC from the L2?

Accountant POV: I foresee this becoming relevant to financial disclosures of businesses using Bitcoin L2s.

OWNING IT: RESPONSIBLY BUILDING IN BITCOIN

Bitcoin's open protocol invites innovation while placing the onus on personal responsibility and shared accountability. The concept of "coopetition" was raised - builders collaborate while competing within the Bitcoin space to grow its value. Unlike Web2 which relies on regulators, Bitcoinâs free market demands that builders be accountable, with ethical responsibility replacing regulatory oversight.

Web3 leaders coming to Bitcoin must be of good character and reputation but also recognise their duty to build responsibly and ethically. As free market maximalists, we believe the market, not regulators, should enforce this accountability.

Experimentation is key to achieving the 10,000X growth needed to scale a global Bitcoin financial system, with L2's enabling expansion while Bitcoinâs core principles are safeguarded

BNS, THE ORIGINAL ONCHAIN NAMING SYSTEM

The Bitcoin Naming System (BNS) is a truly open, decentralised naming system rooted in Satoshi's vision, originally on Namecoin and Bitcoin L1. Similar to ENS on Eth, BNS allows for tokenised ID's like electrafrost.btc instead of long wallet addresses. The BNS V2.0 upgrade introduces new features and namespaces for marketplace compatibility. 

As we stacks sats for the Internet of Money, could linking traditional domains like electrafrost.com with decentralised IDs through .Locker be the breakthrough orange pill for 370m domain registrants? ð¤

#BitcoinNashville Bitcoin Builders Conference Highlights Pt 1 of 4: All Roads Lead to Bitcoin! As a digital accountant and early web adopter, I'm researching emerging applications in the Bitcoin ecosystem to stay at the forefront of my field because BITCOIN IS THE INTERNET WITH MONEY. 

Bitcoin is the most sound asset in the digital world. Now a borderless financial system of payments, savings, investments, mortgages and digital commerce is being built around this pristine asset.

What does that mean to an accountant/business adviser?
Bitcoin integrates financial capabilities directly into the internet, offering transformative potential for BTC-holding businesses of all sizes to scale competitively. Stacks blockchain for instance, turns a bitcoin store-of-value into programmable digital money at the Layer 2 level. 

Tim Draper stated that "Stacks will be the next Microsoft", believing in Stacks' potential to boom in the blockchain space, much like Microsoft did with software. Draper sees Stacks, a layer 2 bringing smart contracts and dApps to Bitcoin, as a transformative technology to drive widespread adoption of Bitcoin-based applications. 

Stacks leverages Bitcoin's security through its Proof of Transfer (PoX) consensus mechanism and introduces Clarity smart contracts for predictable and secure dApps. Draper envisions Stacks creating an ecosystem similar to Microsoft's, attracting developers and users, to become a cornerstone in the blockchain industry.

Accountants are seeing more clients holding BTC on their balance sheets, and comparing it to digital gold with potential for capital appreciation. Our next step is to understand how this Bitcoin capital can be productively unlocked in various business models. 

Users are coming to Bitcoin with fresh eyes, asking what can be done with this financial asset. Is it too much to ask to borrow or lend our BTC trustlessly? The base capital is there - there's no need to fabricate it with tokens.

In the last 20 years we've guided bricks and mortar business models to adapt to the internet. Next they'll adapt to Bitcoin. Emerging solutions offer users options based on their specific needs for transaction speed, privacy, scalability, and functionality. We must also learn about the risks and trust assumptions down the decentralisation scale.

Our clients may also hold other crypto assets. While Ethereum competes on technical aspects to attract capital, it cannot match Bitcoin's >$1tn market cap. With Layer 2 solutions like Stacks and Lightning Network unlocking that capital by making Bitcoin programmable and vastly transactable, Bitcoin's potential appears magnitudes greater than that of other blockchains. 

If you or your clients hold BTC on your balance sheet - follow this space with me! The more we understand Bitcoin as a technology with programmable digital commodity features, beyond P2P cash and digital gold, the clearer the future of money (and accounting) becomes.

Next post will be about the presentations!

The world changed on Friday, July 19, 2024. People became very aware of the BSOD, also known as the "Blue Screen of Death." But why? Over 8 million computer systems were crippled due to CrowdStrike, a cybersecurity company that is, or was, the leading choice in the industry for many companies all over the world to be safeguarded against hacks. Since 2011, they’ve become a very important player in the tech world. As software companies often do, they pushed the update that was designed to prevent things related to external cyberattacks. Sounds great, right?

Well, this update triggered a logic error that made the native operating system crash. This operating system crash only impacted companies running on Microsoft Windows, not Mac or Linux. This wrecked banks, airlines, gas stations, point-of-sale systems, and more. A loved one from South Africa couldn’t access their bank account. A friend traveling from Washington, DC, had their flight delayed for hours. The biggest threat to a system is its point of failure. CrowdStrike was a single point of failure within many systems around the world. That’s a problem.

This made me wonder: what would have happened if the system was set up in a decentralized way? Bitcoin is not only digital gold, a medium of exchange, immutable, and divisible, but it is also OSS, also known as open source software, that anyone can build upon. A decentralized system like Bitcoin has a key benefit as an alternative system; the lack of a central point of failure. Bitcoin's decentralized network is not controlled by any single entity, so an issue with one component would not bring down the entire system. This may make Bitcoin more resilient to the kind of widespread outage caused by the CrowdStrike update. Bitcoin layers such as Stacks or even infrastructure layers like Arch Network may be able to scale Bitcoin to higher heights.

The acts of few affect the many. This is true, not only in life but also in tech.

If AWS goes down, so do a large number of websites. If JP Morgan fails, this will ripple to many more banks. If America falls, it will have consequences on a global level, especially when looking at monetary policy. The world is getting crazy, so we have to do what I call “Planning to Z”. What are the most important aspects of your life? I frame things like Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs. While this addresses the internal aspects of needs, based on a human’s perspective, I’ve created a way for people to assess their own “System of Life”. What do you need to ensure that you have, no matter what comes in this ever-changing world?

Click here to read the rest of "Planning to Z" from my weekly newsletter, "Christopher's Perceptions": https://lnkd.in/dEuyZUa9

As a kid in the 80s, I joined my parents at anti nuclear war protests, terrified by mushroom clouds and atomic bomb aftermath on TV. In 2003, my accounting boss brought his kid and we marched with thousands against the Gulf War. In 2021, we stood up against government overreach during the pandemic and faced police brutality. 

Now in 2024, I'm wondering if we'll protest wars when Australian laws can restrict our right to protest under the guise of national security, and THE ADF HAS KILLER DRONES. 😨 

(I don't normally watch TV but saw this news story in the hotel gym today)

As an accountant, I'm preparing to report on companies' ESG scorecards, reflecting their environmental, social, and governance performance. Since KPMG has already educated us on the positive ESG environmental impacts of Bitcoin mining, it's time to broaden the conversation to include how Bitcoin can also support the social aspects of ESG. 

Unlike fiat currencies, whose value is often backed by governments' ability to print money and, by extension, fund wars, Bitcoin's value is backed by proof-of-work and scarcity. Understanding this fundamental difference changes how we think about ethical financial practices.

"Fiat currency was invented to fund the cause of war." Robert Kennedy Jnr

"The goal is to have endless wars. Not successful ones." Julian Assange.

For the first time ever we can demonetise wars by using Bitcoin. Bitcoin prevents governments from funding their ambitions with inflation. 

By printing money out of thin air, governments diminish the value of our fiat currency, which makes life harder for most. A fiat currency enables money printing and tends to support long and costly wars. Bitcoin makes war less durable... because noone can print more of it.

By incorporating Bitcoin into our lives and businesses, and discussing the benefits of a global reserve asset with a fixed supply and decentralised nature, we can promote a fairer financial system that resists inflationary pressures and can bring greater social stability and trust in those who hold public office.

Displaying Bitcoin on the balance sheet can be a strong statement of support for a more sustainable and ethical financial future.

Who's brave enough to protest now? If we feel insecure about our right to protest, it's time we rethink our approach. Integrating Bitcoin into corporate financial practices is another way to limit the unchecked financial power of governments and support a transparent, peaceful and ethical financial system for all. 

With sustainability reporting (ESG), the accounting industry has an opportunity right now to stand for a future where financial practices align with our values of peace and social justice.

Just my 2 sats on 
hashtag
#tokenization and 
hashtag
#realworldassets... I have immense respect for Rajat Soni, CFA and his insights. However, I believe that limiting our perspective to Bitcoin as a monetary system alone will not bridge the wealth divide. 

We must redefine all value in digital forms, on open and permissionless systems, and ensure that accountants can fairly represent this new paradigm. 

Bitcoin laid the foundation, and The Bitcoin Standard supports it, but for us all to truly showcase our Net Assets, we need to tokenize all forms of income-generating assets on blockchain—be they tangible or intangible, whole or fractionalized - with Bitcoin as Unit of Account.

The privileged typically own properties and companies. Many others, however, possess only their ideas, reputations, and perhaps a home that isn't owned by a bank. It is both fair and democratic to publicly recognize and account for the value everyone brings to society, individually or collectively.

Tokenization on a public ledger allows us to invest in each other's potential and shared needs - whether it's art, business ventures, credentials, or my future dream retirement village with food gardens.

Accountants, business advisers and financial planners must strive to understand and embrace this shift. Them failing to do so holds everyone back.

This is an important moment in our development.  

Client Agent Linking is a tough transition but the ATO knows the imminent threats. CAL has to be done for our protection. Nevertheless, large centralised data silos like the ATO's, Xero's etc will still be targeted and compromised.

It's one of the many reasons that our modern digital age will need Bitcoin (using layers and chains that leverage Bitcoin's security model) - to secure the internet against bots and Al.

ANY transaction or website will have to be protected/ guaranteed with a micro-payment, acting as a proof-of-humanity.

Cyber warfare will be the next frontier, whether we like it or not. And we will be at the front line.

Other crypto and blockchain platforms like Ethereum will also be used, however Bitcoin-level security can prevent a wide range of AI-driven cyber threats, secure AI operations, and verify AI outputs.

As always, I'm most interested in the impacts of Bitcoin, blockchain and AI convergence on our profession and if anyone else is researching this please reach out to me. The future accounting textbooks for the digital asset revolution will be written by graduates of The Accountant Quits' Crypto Accounting Academy. We explored ACCOUNTING FOR ON-CHAIN TRANSACTIONS in Week 2, with Bassil Eid from detof, using his intuitive crypto subledger tool [B](https://www.linkedin.com/company/alwaysbebreezing/)reezing

"For accountants who manage crypto business clients. Not individuals who need a tax return or crypto tracking."

To compare the differences in Balance Sheet presentation, exercises were:
1. Using Xero and creating Chart of Accounts by Token
2. Using Quickbooks and creating Chart of Accounts by Token
3. Using Xero and creating Chart of Accounts by Wallets
4. Using Quickbooks and creating Chart of Accounts by Wallets

We created our test companies in the accounting software with some additions to the COA for crypto, set up the company's digital wallets in the subledger, pulled in some test transactions from the blockchains, checked balances on the block explorers, connected Xero/QB to Breezing crypto subledger, created some accounts rules and mapping, started categorising on-chain transactions (send, receive, swap and internal), synced them to the accounting software and examined the resulting journals. 
Hmmm!

Discussing the pros and cons of booking journal entries by token view vs wallet view was really interesting to me, because I've tended towards a wallet view with my Chart of Accounts. I guess that's just how I self-taught and it was habit. Likewise, setting up Bank Accounts in Xero for crypto rather than Asset Accounts has been the default approach adopted from previous crypto subledger implementations. 

This lesson was valuable to me because it got me thinking about why, and for which types of business operations, different approaches could be appropriate. I think also depends on the software you're using, and what the clients wants to disclose on their financial reports. I'd like to know what you think too!

I'll be pondering all this as I spend the weekend pulling apart my own company's crypto accounting (ElectraFi is my guinea pig) and getting myself up to speed for Part 2 of this habit-challenging module next week!

I just renewed my 16+ year membership with The Tax Institute. As we await the release of the Board of Taxation's much-anticipated report on the tax treatment of digital assets and transactions, I'm hopeful that it will pave the way for substantive tax legislation that I can use to advise clients with. 

Since 2021, we've waited for much-needed clarity and to move beyond the current interpretations of existing laws by the ATO (and all their non-binding website guidance that we take screen shots of) which often lead to complexities and inconsistencies, resulting in inequitable tax outcomes for crypto taxpayers.

The ATO's guidance for crypto users has been insufficient, primarily focusing on basic fungible tokens and investment activities, which does not cover the broader and rapidly evolving digital asset economy. The tax treatment of crypto assets should not be left solely to ordinary tax principles. 

The current tax treatment of crypto assets is not clear and understood under Australian tax law, leading to significant uncertainty and hesitation among tax practitioners and the public. 

I believe, as tax professionals, we are social advocates. Many of us are advocating for a specific statutory regime or framework for the emerging crypto and digital asset economy, to provide clarity and ensure appropriate tax outcomes.

I thought about quitting the tax profession last year, out of frustration with the growing tardiness of Australia's tax system and pain of dealing with the ATO. In my accounting practice, because we manage our clients' affairs all year round and, with forecasting, tax planning is a continual element of our monthly reporting. Tax is just another business expense we manage. 

However, there's more work to do here for the crypto and blockchain industry.

Sometimes I think about replacing the Tax Practitioners Board with a Decentralised Autonomous Organisation (DAO) of tax professionals, and potentially the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) as well. It would be a great project for CTAs in blockchain! 

As such, I figured that it is imperative that I remain a member of the TTI for life.

It's very tempting for teams to rush work at this time of year.. and invariably it comes back to bite us a year or two later... discovering our stuff ups and having to do unpaid rectification work and amendments at the worst possible time. Fortunately our nice clients accept they make things difficult for us by being late, and I haven't needed to say this.

BITCOIN HALVING TODAY! This is a significant event that ensures deflationary effects on our future currencies built on Bitcoin layers ð° 

Bitcoin has a four-year appreciation cycle, and itâs marked by the Halving.

As Bitcoin approaches its 4th halvingâexpected today, just 30 blocks awayâhere's a TLDR version of a ZeroHedge article.

**Bitcoin Halving Simplified**: Every 210,000 blocks, Bitcoin mining rewards halve, slowing the creation of new coins to prolong the mining systemâs viability. This event, occurring roughly every four years, is designed to create scarcity similar to precious metals like gold.

**Effects on Bitcoinâs Value**: Halving typically triggers a price surge in Bitcoin by reducing supply and increasing scarcity, similar to past events where the minerâs reward drop heightened Bitcoin's perceived value.

**Bitcoin vs. Gold**: After the halving, Bitcoin's inflation rate will fall below goldâs near-2% annual increase, suggesting Bitcoin might be a more stable value reserve than traditional assets.

**Future Value Projections**: The article suggests that continual reductions in Bitcoin issuance could drastically boost its price, especially as daily rewards are projected to fall below one Bitcoin by 2060.

**Cultural and Economic Implications**: More than a technical adjustment, the halving signifies a shift towards a decentralized monetary system, potentially redistributing economic power from traditional currencies to Bitcoin.

I'm currently reviewing crypto accounting subledger / back office software for crypto exchanges. As business accountants since 2013 we've typically dealt with clients' crypto payments and reserves, since 2021 with collectable and utility NFTs, web3 start-ups and DAOs, reconciling on-chain and off-chain data using tools that connect with their accounting system. 

Now looking at custody of customer assets and associated liability, we're aware of more complex accounting principles and regulatory risks at play. So it's great to learn how crypto ERP software does FBO accounting ("For Benefit Of" accounts).

Most assume that crypto tax software can do the job. For a small operator with occasional crypto payments, it can, as long as you prepare manual journals in Excel and accounting software. Your double-entry accounting time really blows out once DeFi, NFTs, smart contracts enter the business operations... with multiple wallets to keep track of, and different accounting methods and cost basis. 

That's when a crypto subledger tool becomes essential to automate all this at scale - with consistency and features for internal controls, for mapping all the transactions to the Chart of Accounts in Xero or QB, and running reports. With the right tax reporting functions, no need to keep two sets of accounts.

Founders don't always start off with great wallet hygiene. Tracking revenue separately to treasury activities is a challenge, and multiple data sources require investigation. So setting up an accounting system for a client also involves working with them to update internal finance policies and processes.

Once all the integrations and configurations are complete, import the data, check it and reconcile (sorry, the software doesn't do it all for us!).... and THEN we can streamline a repeatable process for producing an accurate P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow, Budget and Forecast reporting pack EVERY MONTH... with relative ease.

This demonstrates to investors and regulators that you have control over the finances. Every month - check data, reconcile, manual journals, generate reports, analyse and present. That's what it's all about... unless you're FTX!

For a crypto exchange, the crypto accounting software needs extremely high volume transaction capabilities, with separate parts for tracking customer assets and the company's assets, and deeply granular reporting for auditing and compliance with accounting standards.

Whether it's a start-up crypto exchange or an e-commerce operation accepting crypto payments - systemising this in Excel is a wildly inefficient and error-prone way to handle digital asset transactions. When our clients are open to using a robust and scalable ERP system then it's a joy!

We've helped to onboard clients to several different crypto subledger accounting systems, reviewed others, and found that their niche functionalities vary. Which crypto accounting tools have you used and what were your biggest implementation challenges?

Silly Facebook won't let me share the article. Cryptocurrency payments come to Adelaide Oval in Victoria! Rob Green thanks for letting me know about this. 

I thought it was interesting because there has been a bit of hoo-hah protest over cash not being accepted at these events anymore... so now they can pay with e-cash instead! 

So - who knows the rules on personal use CGT exemption and how it applies to paying for things with crypto? Need a separate wallet that's freshly funded for private spending, to avoid CGT. So ask your clients if they maintain a wallet that's only for private spending and wasn't funded from an investment wallet transfer, generally speaking. 

Would you buy a beer and pie with your crypto wallet?

The task of integrating crypto transactions into standard accounting systems has been a persistent challenge for accountants, Web3 start-ups and crypto company CFOs alike. At Electra Frost Accounting my original practice we first encountered Bitcoin in 2013, with an animated film production client who was paying overseas artists with BTC. In these early days prior to any official tax and accounting guidance, we exercised professional judgment to apply FOREX accounting. We were excited by this innovative new form of money and curiously prepared complex Excel spreadsheets with currency conversions to post journals into Xero. Crypto accounting subledger tools sit between the blockchain and your accounting system. They import transaction data directly from wallets and exchanges, apply accounting rules and cost basis methods, generate journals, and push reconciled entries into Xero, QuickBooks, or NetSuite. This eliminates the manual CSV work and reduces the error rate significantly. The key distinction from crypto tax software is that subledger tools maintain the double-entry integrity required for proper financial statements - not just tax calculations. For a Web3 start-up or a business with significant crypto activity, this is the difference between financial reporting that can be audited and a spreadsheet that might pass a basic tax return.
title: Automating Your Way Out of Manual Crypto Accounting

text:
I'm currently reviewing crypto accounting subledger / back office software for crypto exchanges. As business accountants since 2013 we've typically dealt with clients' crypto payments and reserves, since 2021 with collectable and utility NFTs, web3 start-ups and DAOs, reconciling on-chain and off-chain data using tools that connect with their accounting system. 

Now looking at custody of customer assets and associated liability, we're aware of more complex accounting principles and regulatory risks at play. So it's great to learn how crypto ERP software does FBO accounting ("For Benefit Of" accounts).

Most assume that crypto tax software can do the job. For a small operator with occasional crypto payments, it can, as long as you prepare manual journals in Excel and accounting software. Your double-entry accounting time really blows out once DeFi, NFTs, smart contracts enter the business operations... with multiple wallets to keep track of, and different accounting methods and cost basis. 

That's when a crypto subledger tool becomes essential to automate all this at scale - with consistency and features for internal controls, for mapping all the transactions to the Chart of Accounts in Xero or QB, and running reports. With the right tax reporting functions, no need to keep two sets of accounts.

Founders don't always start off with great wallet hygiene. Tracking revenue separately to treasury activities is a challenge, and multiple data sources require investigation. So setting up an accounting system for a client also involves working with them to update internal finance policies and processes.

Once all the integrations and configurations are complete, import the data, check it and reconcile (sorry, the software doesn't do it all for us!).... and THEN we can streamline a repeatable process for producing an accurate P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow, Budget and Forecast reporting pack EVERY MONTH... with relative ease.

This demonstrates to investors and regulators that you have control over the finances. Every month - check data, reconcile, manual journals, generate reports, analyse and present. That's what it's all about... unless you're FTX!

For a crypto exchange, the crypto accounting software needs extremely high volume transaction capabilities, with separate parts for tracking customer assets and the company's assets, and deeply granular reporting for auditing and compliance with accounting standards.

Whether it's a start-up crypto exchange or an e-commerce operation accepting crypto payments - systemising this in Excel is a wildly inefficient and error-prone way to handle digital asset transactions. When our clients are open to using a robust and scalable ERP system then it's a joy!

ElectraFi has helped to onboard clients to several crypto subledger accounting systems, reviewed others, and found that their niche functionalities vary. Which crypto accounting tools have you used and what were your biggest implementation challenges?

**What is the true valuation of Bitcoin? **

Ever find yourself in a debate about Bitcoin's worth with friends or family who are yet to study crypto world? They often mention financial experts who claim, "Bitcoin's value is merely what someone else is willing to pay." But as accountants we know there's more to measuring value.

Beyond this simplified view, multiple models can offer insight into Bitcoin's valuation:

1. **Stock to Flow Model:** This considers Bitcoin's available supply against its annual production. Historically, it's been a solid indicator of Bitcoin's price trends. It looks at two attributes: stock (circulating supply) and flow (new supply). (see image below)
1. **Comparables:** Similar to how we assess real estate by comparing it with similar properties, Bitcoin can be evaluated against the price of other similar assets to gauge its worth.
1. **Metcalfeâs Law:** The value of a network, like Bitcoin, grows exponentially with each new user, increasing its potential transactions and overall worth, especially now with added network effects of Layer 2s (anchored to Bitcoin)
1. **Total Addressable Market (TAM):** Bitcoin is often compared to gold, with its market potential pegged at around $14.5 trillion. So if everyone swapped their gold for Bitcoin...
1. **Cost of Production:** Research shows Bitcoin's price tends to align with its production costs, though this doesn't account for its market demand. Commodities don't trade at their production cost.
1. **Revenue Multiples:** The Bitcoin network generates significant revenues for miners who secure it with their computing power.  Today miners made ~$75MM in revenues. For context, Nvidia's valuation is at 35x its sales.

Does this cover all the valuation methods, or is there a model you believe paints a better picture of Bitcoin's value?

Controversial tax agent services idea - help me think this over, please, Brainies! 

I don't do tax returns anymore (except for some old company clients). I just don't want to deal with any of the client administration, tax forms or ATO. I did the whole tax practice thing for 20 years and am over it all... especially the ATO. ð

But I like helping people and am a good adviser.
And people still want my help with their tax returns. 

One prospect I just rejected has asked me if I could step him through MyGov ITR over Zoom screenshare - his tax return is basic except for the crypto sole trader business schedule. 

At first I said no then I thought... hang on, if I just sort out his crypto tax software reports, then instruct him to fill out and lodge his own return, then my job is done in a meeting, without even filling out a form! 

I'd never have to worry about him again. I wouldn't have to deal with any of his ATO correspondence or future due dates. Or get audit insurance, or be obliged to reply to his email quick questions.

If he gets audited (which is probably less likely on MyGov than with a tax agent these days hah!), then he can engage me for representation, which would cost a lot more.

But I would not be representing him through the year. I would only be advising. 

What if I did this with all clients? ð¤

Never actually engage them with my agent number on a tax return. 

I could earn $300+ an hour and not have any unbillable time doing tax agent practice management. I'd have no ATO client list. 

I wonder what the engagement letter would look like. ð¤

During a recent Institute of Professional Accountants Town Hall Webinar, it was revealed that many professional accountants are not fulfilling their CPE/CPD requirements due to lack of time and cost of training. I proposed publicly displaying CPE/CPD achievements and logged hours to encourage compliance and prioritise continuous learning. By leveraging public blockchain technologies, accountants can establish trust, prove their value, and ensure transparency and integrity in the profession. What I am describing is CREDU - a decentralised credentialling system for accountants built on Bitcoin and open blockchain protocols. CREDU would allow practitioners to log their CPD activities on-chain, have them verified by peers and professional bodies, and display them publicly in a way that is tamper-proof and portable. The credential would belong to the practitioner, not the institution that awarded it. This is a significant departure from current CPD systems, which are institution-controlled, not portable, and not publicly verifiable. CREDU would also change the economics of CPD by enabling a value-for-value model in which practitioners pay for content with attention and contribution rather than subscription fees.
title: Breaking New Ground in Accounting CPE & CPD with a Decentralised Application - Part 1

In the vibrant world of Bitcoin NFTs, two innovative protocols have emerged 
hashtag
#BRC20 Ordinals & 
hashtag
#SRC20 Stamps. 

Let's unravel their differences in this post! 

2/ ⚙️Technical Foundations


hashtag
#Ordinals embed data onto individual Satoshis, the smallest unit of Bitcoin, using the BRC20 protocol. This allows for unique tokens with digital content attached, creating non-fungible digital collectibles on the Bitcoin blockchain.


hashtag
#Stamps, on the other h&, leverage the SRC20 protocol, embedding data directly into the UTXO set. This ensures permanence within the blockchain & introduces semi-fungibility, allowing users to issue different types of digital assets.

3/ 🔐 Security & Immutability


hashtag
#Ordinals face potential data pruning, impacting long-term security. The BRC20 protocol's reliance on nodes raises concerns about data loss & alteration, as the decentralized network may prune old witness data.


hashtag
#Stamps, by embedding data in the UTXO set, offer enhanced security & immutability. This approach ensures that once data is on the blockchain, it remains permanently preserved, immune to pruning or manual alterations.

4/ 🚀 Use Cases


hashtag
#Ordinals are suitable for applications where data permanence is not critical. The potential for data pruning may limit their use in scenarios requiring enduring data integrity.


hashtag
#Stamps shine in applications demanding enduring data integrity. Legal documents, certificates, & historical records benefit from the immutability of Stamps, ensuring long-term reliability.

5/ ⚖️ Regulatory Compliance

The potential for data loss & alterations in 
hashtag
#Ordinals may raise regulatory compliance concerns, especially in industries requiring tamper-proof records.


hashtag
#Stamps, with their permanence, provide a robust solution for regulatory compliance. The tamper-proof nature aligns with legal standards, making them a reliable choice in industries where data integrity is paramount.

6/ 📊 Scalability

The Ordinalq BRC20 protocol, relying on nodes to store witness data, may contribute to blockchain bloat, potentially impacting 
hashtag
#scalability. Scalability concerns arise as adoption grows.


hashtag
#Stamps, designed to occupy minimal space in the UTXO set, contribute to efficient scalability. This minimizes the impact on the overall efficiency of the Bitcoin network, addressing concerns related to blockchain bloat.

7/ ✨ Potential for Innovation

The 
hashtag
#Ordinals/BRC20 protocol offers an innovative way to tag & track individual 
hashtag
#Satoshis, providing granularity in data embedding. However, the potential for data pruning introduces a trade-off in terms of permanence. 
hashtag
#Innovation


hashtag
#Stamps bring innovation with semi-fungibility, allowing users to issue various types of digital assets. The direct embedding into the UTXO set ensures a secure & lasting record of ownership, fostering new possibilities.
