A proposed merger of Australia's three major accounting bodies signals a move towards further centralisation, a trend viewed as outdated and futile by leading technologists in the digital economy. A more democratic and innovative approach is emerging: forming a Decentralised Autonomous Organisation. A DAO is a significant departure from traditional unions and mergers. Rather than amalgamating Australia's three major professional accounting bodies into a single centralised entity, a DAO would merge the members of these bodies into a member-governed digital organisation. This approach includes the existing professional bodies in a non-hierarchical DAO structure, without replacing or controlling them. The DAO model enables accountants to retain their professional designations and institutional affiliations while participating in a new layer of decentralised governance that gives members more direct influence over the standards, education, and advocacy that affect their practices. The merger proposal treats centralisation as the solution to fragmentation. The DAO model treats decentralisation as the solution to both fragmentation and the unresponsiveness that comes with centralised institutional control. If we are going to create a dominant structure for the accounting profession, it should be a structure that its members control directly.
title: DAO vs Merger: Merge the Accountants, not their Accounting Bodies

text:
My first LinkedIn article! Instead of merging Australia's 3 major accounting bodies, why don't we merge the members in a digital organisation? If we're creating a monopoly, make it a decentralised monopoly! 

It's a perfect context to consider a Decentralised Autonomous Organisation (DAO) for governance and directing more value to members.

A digital organisation on a network model is destined to be a monopoly, and (referencing Paul Brody from EY, 'Ethereum for Business') if we are going to build business models on a monopoly network, the most public, permissionless, secure and decentralised monopoly is the only kind worth having. The necessary functionality may now be achieved through the use of layered solutions, protocols, and proposals built on top of the Bitcoin network.

What do you think? 🤓 

Joe Kaleb Jason Andrew Damian Lloyd Joni Pirovich Harrison Dell Timothy Munro David Boyar Heather Smith FCCA, FCA Karen Moloney Andrew Conway FIPA Yadanar Moe Yu Valentine Tse Eddie Griffith Sam Kendall Dr Aneesha Varghese Cowan Adam Thorpe Jennifer Glisson Mani Duggal Kosala (Kosy) Aravinda Nola Luyten FIPA Vicki Stylianou Arthur Burt, and thank you William Saunders for the pep talk about Digital Organisations :)

✍ I really need to blog consistently to share ideas effectively with my target audience. So I've made a head start on my new year's resolution to publish a 500-word post every week, by joining the Bitcoin Writers Cohort! 

The Bitcoin Writers Cohort helps Bitcoin L2 builders wanting to amplify their message, created in partnership with Taptive and led by Grant Nissly. I'm amongst some incredibly talented creators here, and we are all excited to write about the things we're most passionate about. 🧡 📙 🙌 

 👇 Here are some of the Week 1 topics from our cohort. I'll post links to their blogs in the comments below, as they're published, and share the links on my Substack blog too (subscribe link in comments)

🤔 My topic is 'DAO vs Merger' - in response to a proposal to merge the 3 major accounting bodies in Australia. I'll explain the benefits of an alternative member-governed digital organisation model that merges the members, not the accounting bodies. Trying to do this in 500 words (instead of 2000) is my big challenge... but with a publishing deadline now it'll get done!

Stay tuned 📻
ATO, ASIC, meeting no-showers, anyone?  Only a few weeks to go. Renew your membership now! ð¤ ð«

Financial advisers cannot recommend 
hashtag
#bitcoin because, like crypto, it is not classified as a financial product. As such, it isn't covered by their professional indemnity insurance policies and that precludes recommendations in an advice document. A financial adviser might discuss pros and cons of Bitcoin with their clients but will avoid making recommendations. Ask them what they've read lately to see if they're curious and keeping up to date.

Tune into this X (Twitter) Space next Thursday at lunhctime'ish... AND PLEASE SHARE ð 

I'll be speaking with Californian Fractional CFO and Financial Controller in for Bitcoin Web3 businesses, Frederick Martinez, about how a decentralised digital organisation of accountants collaborating globally would work. He had some pretty great ideas when we spoke yesterday so we decided to share our conversation and invite others in.
https://twitter.com/i/spaces/1YpKkwPpqMYKj?s=20

Edit to add - rescheduled to a better time 10am Tues 19 Dec as I am losing my voice today and won't be able to speak tonight ð

.........

ð Tune in at 6.30pm AEST tonight for the followup from SBAABTA DAO education webinar. Talking about the real purpose of a Digital Organisation built on the values and community spirit of this group and the most cutting edge technologies to solve some of the problems of our professional bodies that we, as members, complain about.  We'll talk about:

1ï¸â£ Joe Kaleb's post on LinkedIn about merging the 3 accounting bodies.

A merger is a further centralisation and with potential for monopolisation. Whereas, a decentralised model led by members is the opposite but tackles the same problems that a merger means to solve.

A DAO would be a merging of the 3 accounting bodies MEMBERS, not the 3 accounting bodies themselves. 

A DAO would INCLUDE the professional bodies and associations in a completely member-governed digital organisation. So it doesn't replace them, just gives us more influence over them.

---------------------------------------------

2ï¸â£ What does SBAABTA (this group) do for you that your professional body doesn't do.

So many strong comments in that thread remind us that if a new Digital Organisation that decentralises our professional associations is based on this group, then it mustn't destroy a beautiful thing. How can it enhance it?

---------------------------------------------

3ï¸â£ "AI will replace all accountants except for the small number employed by Big4" - the argument given by the start-up accelerator that rejected my project for global accountants.

I don't think we should dismiss this out of hand, even if they were fools with no idea about accounting and probably the next FTX.

We are stronger in numbers and can use technology to organise ourselves and harness our own content for AI benefits that flow to us, rather than to the top, so we can keep supporting the small business economy.

---------------------------------------------

4ï¸â£ Mark Newman's post about the world getting so complex that we need to stick to our areas of expertise, for our wellbeing. 

By the same token, we have to collaborate to remain competitive. Large accounting companies with vast resources of human expertise, BI and AI are eyeing our target markets.

Collectively, we need to compete with them as a highly effective network of niches. So that when you get a client with a winery, you and your AI agent can tap into the WET expertise of John Smith CPA and keep that client.

Freelance economies like this can be created in blockchain based digital organisations, such as a DAO. They are completely member-run - they don't have a central authority such as a franchisor or accounting body. They are decentralised and autonomous, running on smart contracts to distribute value and work amongst its members.
So, yes, business is getting a whole lot harder but if we prioritise our time now to adapting to the RIGHT "future of work" then we can make things better in the next 10 years.

---------------------------------------------

and finally 

5ï¸â£ ETHICS ... who decides where the lines are drawn? We do!

This week members disagreed on how an employee who faked their covid jab certificate should be treated, in particular the ethical ramifications of it. 

DAO voting mechanisms allow members to shape governance of ethical standards within a DAO. It's healthy to respectfully share different views (which this group is so good at!)... but rather than asserting fixed views on ethics and saying we are appalled by each other we could could just anonymously vote.... on a very large scale! 

How that could be done, by design, it just one of the many jobs of the experts in the establishment team of a DAO. So we will look at how a professional services DAO is structured to enact governance.

---------------------------------------------

oh and 6ï¸â£ 

The vision of monetised peer to peer CPD. How we get paid by each other for supporting each other in a Value 4 Value model?

---------------------------------------------

So that'll be me talking for 40-45 mins
With 15-20 mins Q&A's in between topics
It'll be recorded and shared publicly.

Come along and listen if you're curious about the future of digital accounting practice and might want to play a role in shaping this. I'm not a leader, just a facilitator and researcher. Your ideas are important. 

Oh and if that merger pushes ahead ... maybe we'll ask IPA/CA/CPA to consider a DAO instead

Chaotic robots! ð¤ð¾ð¤ð± Feel free to disagree but from an ethical POV I don't think any of us should be promoting any serious use of generative-AI tools in our accounting businesses, when the underlying LLMs could be unsafe and exposing us to unknown risks.

All this "Harnessing the power of AI in your accounting practice!" talk and apps being shared by the new breed of AI experts makes me so nervous. Sure, we have to practice using it, it's going to change everything. But noone is asking -"hey, how do I validate that I can trust this thing to do what I would want to do? ...where's the blockchain traceability?"

Our AI robots need blockchain to engineer trust into them - as a governance layer for AI training data, to either protect and license private data or govern shared data, using a cryptocurrency payment rail and smart contracts agreement layer. 

Artificial intelligence is creative, and smart contracts are secure. We don't let artists do our jobs.

To ensure safety and reliability, blockchain based smart contracts can help filter AI outputs, aligning AI actions with user intentions, crucial for us busy accountants.

Just a thought I had to share...

Will the public will ever change their perception of us as just form-fillers who are an extension of the government? We have such a long way to go, and we need to market ourselves better.

I just had a frustrating interview with a start-up pre-accelerator - about my project - building a decentralised knowledge monetisation protocol for SME accountants. It a complicated idea and it needs work. I understand start-ups and applied for mentoring and guidance with specialist tech. (not keen to give equity, anyhow)

But here's the thing - 

I couldn't get past their misperceptions about us as an industry... basically, all we do and can ever do is reporting the past to government.... and all accounting and tax and advisory work will be done by AI, and unless you're in the top 5% of "CPAs" that the big tier absorbs then you're destined for the AI-era scrap heap. 

Only the large firms will be left standing, I was told. 

Sure, we're probably training that model right now every time we use ChatGPT, for Microsoft's benefit. We should be training LLMs in a decentralised way that will economically benefit us all in future, but we're not learning fast enough how to do that. We're not having the most urgent conversations around AI in that context, I conceded.

But, regardless, millions and millions of small businesses in the world still need the advocacy and guidance of their accountants to navigate doing business digitally... and they need us to be well connected and resourced, industry niched and knowledge-adaptive. 

And millions of accountants need to be publicly validated for their niche credentials and able to monetise their knowledge efficiently, and be influencers in society. People want to work with us and our specialist networks that understand and advocate for them.... not the KPMG-Bot.

They also thought I was too old to have the stamina for a start-up. ð And that as an SME accountant I could never scale globally. ð .... ouch at being so wrongly pigeonholed personally. 

Whatever. That's not worrying me.

What bothered me MOST that they insisted my target market was basically a bunch of old fuddy duddies that was headed for the AI-era scrap heap and so not worth investing in.

And I think  that THIS perception is the problem we need to tackle first, starting in our own minds and OUR visions of the future. And then assert our future roles.

Remember Chris Jordan saying at Xero Con last year "In 3-5 years you need to change your business model". They really believe it's all over for us. They don't see us.

In 2024, what are we going to do about that?

A big week ahead, in Sydney. Exhibiting ElectraFi at Accounting & Business Expo, and joining the conversation at the Crypto Assembly with Blockchain APAC . Steve Vallas has done an amazing job to bring everyone together for this. Looking forward to many rich and productive conversations to drive our blockchain missions forward in 2024! Who's going?

💣 "Hey just putting this out there- since we get more information and support from this group than CPA, IPA, CA - can we set up our own body to compete!" - was posted in a big accountants' Facebook group I'm in.

🎆 I replied "How about a DAO for peer to peer CPD and representation?" It was a tongue in cheek post, but did arouse some hot discussion.

💡 My blog article below sets a theme for 2024, with a diverse group of SMP accountants exploring the idea of a Decentralised Autonomous Professional Association. An open blockchain based protocol addresses our economic, reputational and self-advocacy challenges, not technological ones. It's not so much about the tech.

💌 The response since the webinar has been good. The topic resonated and stirred some clever ideas. Next week I'll share some of the anon. feedback (via my blog) and from January our meetings will be public.

👇 Please subscribe to my blog to see what happens next, and joining the conversation 🤓

Electra Frost created a private online event for Small Business Accountants & Advisers Brain Trust, Australia.
Clients will be building new business models on Bitcoin, crypto and digital assets in 2024... let's get ready!

I hosted an Accountants On-Chain online event prompted by a member post: "since we get more information and support from this group than CPA, IPA, CA - can we set up our own body to compete!" I replied: we can set up a DAO. It won't cost us anything if we contribute know-how and support in a Value 4 Value model. A DAO for accountants would not replace professional bodies. It would give members more direct influence over the profession. It would be member-governed, member-funded, and member-run. It would enable practitioners to collaborate on standards, share knowledge, and advocate collectively without going through institutional gatekeepers. The knowledge that currently lives in private Facebook groups and Slack channels would be on-chain, attributed, and rewarded. The CPD that currently requires expensive subscriptions would be peer-validated and publicly verifiable. The governance that currently happens in board rooms would happen in open votes. This is not a utopian vision. It is a practical application of tools that already exist to a problem that already affects every small practice accountant in Australia.

Despite over $0.5bn lost through MyGov hacker tax fraud since 2021, there's hope for our security. Victor Dominello was NSW MP of Digital last year and spearheaded some cool Web3 privacy tech initiatives to give user-control over ID documentation for rental applications, qualifications and other things. 

Now he's Chair of the Federal Digital ID panel and bringing passkey security to MyGov. Many hold reasonable concerns about government overreach and surveillance with Digitall Identity apps but, to be honest, I worry more about their technology mismanagement that is more likely to subject us to "computer says no" dystopian scenarios. Like we're already a bit familar with as tax agents.

Victor Dominello is a good influence. Web2 (login password honeypots) is dead. Web3 (user owned internet with blockchain) is still early. He's doing Web 2.5 ð

Imagine losing 25 BTC you'd hodl'd since 2012. Poor Rick, I read his tweets... he was sloppy with his self-custody security, stored his seed words on an internet connected device, and got hacked. He was travelling overseas ... and it's tricky to store seed-phrases in a safe place other than your brain when you're on the move 🤢

What self-custody options are left to us?

The Bitcoin Adviser (who cover the story below) offer a collaborative custody model to relieve Bitcoin hodl'ers of the overbearing responsibility of being their own single point of failure. A trade-off of trustlessness for some peace of mind that will surely suit many

Ryder.id have ushered in a new era of self-custody with a groundbreaking TapSafe recovery system. It replaced the complicated seed-phrase process with a simple combination of hardware, mobile app, and social circle. If the first two recovery options fail, you can fall back on a couple of close friends or family to unlock access to your assets. (check out their Kickstarter if interested)

Whether you're more comfortable with paying a fee for a professional service or buying special hardware, taking charge of our financial future on a non-custodial basis is becoming more accessible. And that's a good thing.

The inclination of digital-native consumers is reshaping the expectations around business transactions. These consumers are anticipated to prefer transacting in cryptocurrencies, valuing speed, privacy, and convenience. Australian SMEs and digital businesses will eventually need to adapt their payment systems to accommodate crypto transactions. To understand the crypto space we need to become familiar with new terminology and concepts, just as we did when we first learned to surf the web. ElectraFi expects all currencies to become digital this decade. We need to grasp the difference between centralised and decentralised digital currencies, the applications of blockchain beyond currency, and the regulatory and tax environment that is rapidly developing around them. The key message for accountants: do not wait for clarity before engaging. The clients who most need your help navigating this space are already in it. The accountants who are learning now will be the ones they call.
title: Cryptocurrency — the challenges, the opportunities… and what it really means for your business or accounting practice

Has anyone had good reason to restructure from private company to family trust? 

Client with an online retail operation in a company threw a curve ball at me, wanting me suddenly to restructure it to a trust ...now she's having her second child some business owner has got in her ear about how they're paying less tax with family trusts....nerrr... so I explained the obvious commercial reasons why not (overseas dealings, having to change all the contracts, the costs) and there'd be no tax benefit in distributing retained earnings to her husband who earns over $100K.

 She would pay more tax, not less, and she needs to retain profits to grow her business. She could possibly hold her shares in a family trust instead of as an individual, for future distributions, but theres be a CGT event and there is no apparant need to create a costly group structure. 

But she was very unconvinced! 

What can I send her to read? 
Or should I just refer her to a lawyer to settle her questions?
Thanks :)

I decided to join Blockchain Australia after finding that its Members' Code of Ethical Conduct resonates well with my professional accounting ethics. As a Fellow member of the Institute of Public Accountants, I am bound to abide by principles of professional ethics contained in the IPA's By-laws and APES 110 Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants. As a Registered Tax Practitioner, I must also observe the TASA's Code of Professional Conduct. Having operated a professional accounting practice for more than 15 years, I recently pivoted my practice to focus on Bitcoin and blockchain business and accounting. The alignment between the accounting profession's ethical framework and the blockchain industry's values of transparency, accountability, and trustless verification is not coincidental. Both are fundamentally about maintaining integrity in the recording and reporting of value. The accounting profession and the Bitcoin industry share a core commitment: that the integrity of records matters, that verification should be possible, and that trust should be earned through transparency rather than assumed through authority.
title: Accountants, Bitcoin and Blockchain Industry: Aligned by Ethical Principles

text: I'm a professional accountant mapping the latest maze of Bitcoin, crypto, DeFi, Web3 and blockchain rabbit holes for accountants to confidently navigate. I care strongly about future-proofing my profession and the small businesses that we advocate for, so I'm always looking for the really empowering technology that can make us all more profitable and resilient in the long-term. I've owned an accounting practice for more than 15 years and was an early adopter of digital business technologies. We first put Bitcoin on the balance sheet in 2014 and things have been getting stranger and more exciting ever since. In 2022, I led the Accountants On-Chain knowledge-sharing project with Digital Playhouse Foundation, the charity I co-founded. My revamped accounting practice, ElectraFi, is now continuing that project as a social enterprise and offering helpful services to accountants with crypto business clients.
title: Welcome to the ElectraFi Insider blog!

I've inscribed the www.electrafi.finance company logo as an Ordinal NFT. An Ordinal is like an NFT you can mint directly onto the Bitcoin blockchain, as a complete digital asset file (not a digital representation) embedded within a satoshi. Your Ordinal can be held in a Bitcoin wallet, or transferred using a Bitcoin transaction.

Back in 2012, âcoloured coinsâ was a first approach to add extra data to individual satoshis, representing real world assets on the Bitcoin blockchain, thus was analogous of âcolouringâ them. Due to their limitations, NFTs were invented which instead reside on Ethereum, Solana and other protocols.

This changed with Taproot addresses (start with bc1P) which offer novel smart contract opportunities for Bitcoin addresses, compared to Bitcoin addresses such as Legacy or P2PKH (start with the number 1), P2SH (start with the number 3) and Segwit/Bech32 (start with bc1).

You can use ordinal blockchain explorers such as [https://ordinals.hiro.so/](https://ordinals.hiro.so/) to search for an inscription with a number of criteria; in this case: inscription #29762337

The Bitcoin community has been somewhat divided on whether NFTs on its blockchain are a good thing. While many see them as a positive way to further grow its ecosystem, some âBitcoin maximalistsâ oppose them for taking up block space on the network and making transaction fees more expensive; some hold concerns about causing non-fungibility and tracking of sats.

I appreciate that uploading non-financial data that will be forever included on the Bitcoin blockchain should not be a frivolous undertaking. Ordinal theory is a methodology for individually identifying (with a serial number) and tracking each individual satoshi throughout the Bitcoin coin supply. With around 2 quadrillion sats, very few of these sats have have been inscribed so far. If all the sats had inscriptions, you wouldn't be able to use Bitcoin like cash anymore, right?

While I agree that this isn't the place for monkey JPEG's and vanity projects, I think that immutably storing valuable property and identity data has merit. And with the advent of recursive inscriptions taking ordinals inscriptions to the next level, with complex data sets of audio and video files hosted on Bitcoin, we could be building "websites" on the most immutable, decentralised and censorship-resistant network in the world.

So my humble company logo is just going to lay in wait to see what becomes possible. And if I sell this business or turn it into a DAO, the Ordinal inscription is an included asset.

Some days in Bitcoin I feel like I'm back in 1996, sitting in my boyfriend's attic building a website on his Macintosh... hacking with reckless abandon!
ð

It was great having drinks with the members last night, as I got to investigate their exposures to clients using crypto in new ways including not-for-profit projects, customer rewards tokens and B2B payments. For the most part, not much exposure, so my presentation will focus on what needs to be on the radar of small business accountants and bookkeepers, and how to scope the crypto business activities of clients who do more than speculatively invest. 

Having so many bookkeepers at this event is ideal... because they're often the first to speak with the client before the accountant about how and for what purpose the business is adopting crypto assets. They'll need to lead on complex matters of entity custody, record keeping and internal accounts processes. "Just put it in my accounting software" is no simple request!

I look forward to the Shoebox bookkeepers and tax agents sharing their perspectives on suitable approaches to be taken with small business crypto adoption by considering case studies of Australians using Bitcoin payments and NFTs... the idyllic location might inspire some creative thinking!

Bitcoin's fusion of ancient monetary principles with cutting edge technology, is challenging our notion of what constitutes 'real money'. 

Beyond money, Bitcoin's scalability is ensured as a settlement layer for other economic activities on Layer 2's and sidechains, and through merging with the renewable energy sector. It's not only the world's most widely accepted currency after the US dollar, it also provides a global economic system prioritising security and reliability... for anyone to use... with no CEO, no bank, no controllers... just energy and maths. 

All accountants need to become conversant on these topics. The reason I got my head around blockchain and web3 quickly and understood what makes it valuable is because I studied Bitcoin first. Start with Bitcoin, and the rest falls into place!

Ordinals aren't dead. Digital Artifacts are a new asset class and Bitcoin is now both a store of value and a medium for art.

OCM Dimensions 300, launched just 2 months ago, achieved groundbreaking feats for Ordinals, including pioneering recursive inscriptions, parent-child collection provenance, and on-chain compression. 

Our flagship collection, OCM Genesis, inscribed in early February, will be launching soon and will have a series of innovative features to its name as well.

Bitcoin is the future and we're excited to continue to innovate and create the highest quality Digital Artifacts for our members and collectors.

For more on why we believe in a bright future for Digital Artifacts, read Danny Yang takes in the latest from Now Media in comments!

Can dividends be taken as any kind of property instead of as cash? I believe so but can't find anything to back me up in the tax act and can't remember dealing with this before.

 I have a client who wants to take all his USDC stablecoin out of his company for his big annual dividend, and he doesn't want to off ramp to cash... crypto is property under the tax act...  and this has actually stumped me!

ð

note:
- definition of 'dividend' in subsection 6(1) of the ITAA 1936 has the general effect that any distribution made by a company to any of its shareholders, whether in money or property, and any amount credited by a company to any of its shareholders as shareholders, is a dividend.

but paragraph d) excludes distributions that were debited against an amount credited on the company's share capital account... I don't think that applies here as it's just company retained income reserves parked into USDC

This is why my accounting practice only works with businesses that want the full financial oversight package. It's like having three accountants continually working in your business for 1/4 of the cost of employing one. We save our clients so much money, stress and time so they can focus and invest in what grows profits.

Yet so many business owners think accountants are too expensive and try to manage things themselves all year round... or use a basic bookkeeping service... only seeing their tax agent once a year to rustle together a tax return! 

We've already prepared everyones' tax returns for year end 30 June 2023. All our clients' obligations are planned in advance based on real time data and forecasts, no surprises, everything managed and paid behind the scenes. 

Business owners have no idea what they're missing by not having monthly reporting on performance, budget variances and balances until the cash flow crisis and director penalties bite. 

Three quarters of small business owners stated that, if starting up again, implementating a robust financial management system would be a top priority. Half regretted not engaging an accountant and bookkeeper up front. It's no surprise, really. Running a profitable, legal business is really hard.

If you know a business earning $500K upwards, how we do things differently can make a huge difference to their 1-5 year outcomes and selling price... Please point them my way, I'll demonstrate what full scope accounting services can do for them.

Business owners, stop cringing over Bitcoin. It's great for cross-border payments. Helping a client with her FY2024 budget last week, I flagged she was spending nearly $20K/yr on international transaction fees. I said "Will you consider Bitcoin?" and she became uncomfortable and changed the subject. But I'll bring it up again as her costs increase. It's a no brainer.

An informative report on Bitcoin adoption reshaping cross-border payments, with the Bitcoin blockchain increasingly used for settlement as new technologies provide layers over Bitcoin for low cost and extremely high volume transaction thoroughput. People will be using Bitcoin without knowing it when transacting in their local currencies.

Ernst & Young has built and launched their own decentralised, permissionless Layer 2 (L2) over the public Ethereum Network. 

E&Y's blockchain strategy sends a powerful message to our industry about private/permissioned blockchains - indicating that they're on the wrong side of the fundamental innovation brought forth by *public blockchains.*

Per Paul Brody, E&Y's head of Global blockchain: "we don't see the value proposition in permissioned systems that claim to be decentralized."

If you're not aware, E&Y is the only Big 4 company solely focused on the *public* Ethereum network.

They turn down consulting engagements with anyone building on *private/permissioned* blockchains. Why? Probably because they view it as a short-term money grab.

---

Back to the L2 launch.

In the opinion of Michael Nadeu (the DeFi report), an opinion I share:

 "the existence of *private/permissioned* blockchains is due to the shortcomings of public blockchains *to date.* In particular, scalability and privacy.

"L2s are beginning to solve scalability constraints by batching transactions off-chain, and then recording proofs in bundled transactions to the L1 for final settlement.

"[This is the innovation of Ethereum: a credibly neutral, shared, permissionless global settlement network & database. It's a mouthful. But it's what allows businesses and individuals to engage with each other, and record their economic activity in a shared, neutral database without trusting each other.]

"Privacy is solved by zero-knowledge proofs: the ability to prove that something happened, without revealing the contents of the data."

----

"In essence, this could open the floodgates for enterprises to utilise public networks like Ethereum [ added - and Stacks / Bitcoin] .

E&Y is working on several manufacturing/industrial supply chain use cases.

They forecast 4 billion transactions/day for this use case alone.

And they think that 50% of enterprises will be on *public blockchains* within 10 years â in line with the timeline it took for enterprises to migrate to the cloud."

----

"Crypto" gets dragged through the mud quite a bit (and rightly so).

But in an interesting twist of irony, the ultimate blockchain crime could ultimately be revealed as "private/permissioned blockchain."

Or maybe we end up somewhere in the middle?

What do you think?

#tax #bitcoin #POS #smallbusiness This stallholder at my local markets is using the new Wallet of Satoshi POS.  

So I bought coffee and kombucha from him and had a chat about accounting and tax.. 

My Wallet of Satoshi (WoS) is only used for private purchases, so I ensure that only "fresh" purchases of Bitcoin fund that wallet so as to avoid having to account for CGT. i.e. I do not include transactions from that particular wallet in my tax return.

How? Part of my salary is paid through getpaidinbitcoin.com.au, employer pays AUD and it converts to BTC for me, and I direct a portion of that Bitcoin to my Wallet of Satoshi "hot wallet" each fortnight for "spending sats". 

I can also buy bitcoin directly using my WoS but it's not as economical. 

As soon as I transfer any bitcoin from my investing/savings wallets to this WoS, I believe it would end the wallet's private use exemption from CGT. It's important for consumers to know how to manage wallet hygiene to take advantage of this CGT-free treatment of private btc transactions.

From the merchant's POV, he is only using his WoS for business, registered to his business email account and using the POS built into the app. No private transactions should happen in that wallet. 

He will transfer around 30% of his daily or weekly btc takings to his company's Bitaroo exchange account (Bitaroo is Lightning enabled, so it's just a phone to phone scan of a QR code), where he can convert it to AUD and transfer to the tax savings bank account. This makes sure he sets aside an approximate  amount for GST and taxes at the current exchange rate, to not worry about price volatility. 

He is transferring the balance to a secure "cold wallet" (using Ledger or Trezor) to save up bitcoin as an inflation edge for his business. He has documentation of the hardware device being company property (to avoid Director loan Div 7A implications). These savings must be reflected on the balance sheet at cost, not fair value.

To do his accounts, he can export a CSV of the  transactions from his WoS, which will have to be calculated into AUD value to be journal's into his accounting software. 

In the USA, there are USD based services that connect Lightning PoS to Quickbooks. I'm not sure how to automate the process for an Australian merchant using Xero. How would you handle this?

The problem with enterprise blockchain, is it did not start with the new incentive model for the internet as part of a new digital economy.

This incentive model is based around the original and rightful owner controlling their pseudo-anonymous reputation and value used as a currency in an atomic swap or trading exchange of an NFT (or FT) representing ownership in an asset born on the internet or with an abstraction layer to the real world. 

In this way value and ownership are transferred at the same time increasing the velocity of money. These exchanges can also be made for the cost of an email eliminating the 2% rent taken by traditional payment rails. 

This grows the size of the digital economy and supports micro transactions need to reward sharing of data (eg from IoT) to drive AI apps. This is the role of Autonomous Economic Agents (AEAâs)

Anti-crypto is anti-competitive. Competition leads to lower prices, more choice and better products for consumers. Those innovating with crypto and blockchain use cases are driving greater value and protections to us than ever before, financially and personally. 

Examples of anti-competitive behaviour include cartel conduct, anti-competitive agreements, exclusionary provisions (boycotts), misuse of market power and exclusive dealings. In Australia, we ban business behaviours that damage competition.

.... and got some basic answers like âmeans of exchangeâ, âhow we measure wealthâ. But what is money, REALLY? â

ð§
Before bitcoin, virtually no one asked this. Bitcoin is hard to understand because money is hard to understand. But that is because few people have ever questioned money at a fundamental level â what it is, and what makes it valuable. 

So we talked about how Bitcoin derives more value than Fiat or Gold - through its durability, portability, divisibility, fungibility, scarcity, and acceptability - to be potentially treated as money. Sound money. HARD money. Its Satoshi denominations and fixed supply ensure it can support adoption by billions of people and no one can print more of it. 

ð

A book about Bitcoin that makes no mention of Bitcoin. ð¤ "The Price of Tomorrow ...Why Deflation is the Key to an Abundant Future"

What are you reading?

What is money? Money is technology. It's information on a database, with currency as monetary value assigned to the data. Fiat currency's value is based on IOU's, backed only by our trust in the government that issues it. 

Whereas Bitcoin's superior value proposition is absolute mathematical scarcity, achieved by consensus in a sufficiently decentralised network... no trust required. 

Revolutionary concepts of hard money challenge every professional person in finance. But we need to spend time learning how Bitcoin works if we are to maintain our intellectual credibility and integrity this decade. Laughing and dismissing Bitcoin risks one appearing foolish. 

Read as much as you possibly can so we can constructively debate what's in our own and others' best interests. I recommend books like The Bitcoin Standard and The Price of Tomorrow, for the economics. But understanding a bit about the technology of decentralisation is equally important.

FYI 
Owning crypto-assets means managing your private keys. The 12-24 word seed phrase gives you exclusive access to your private key. If youâre not owning (i.e. exclusively accessing) your private keys, you donât really own your crypto. By using a Ledger, Trezor or other trusted hardware device sold by the manufacturer, youâre always in complete control of your private keys, meaning that you can recover your funds & assets at any moment if needed, independently of any third party, including the manufacturer of the device.

This very well researched article by an electrical engineer dispels much of the misinformation spread by Bitcoin's powerful competitors. Explains the purpose of Bitcoin, why it uses energy, its efficient scaling pattern, how it uses otherwise-wasted energy, how it's bootstrapping sustainable energy infrastructure in developing countries, and more.

Proof-of-Work makes Bitcoin secure, decentralised and socially ethical. Bitcoin survives by not using Proof-of-Stake. Arguments from environmental advocates for Bitcoin to move to PoS should be scrutinised against this data.

Basically - If the agent requires you to prove that you earn $100K and your data under your control can validate you earned $100K, all they see is a TICK. 

NOT to be confused with CENTRALISED digital identity apps that store your records/data on someone else's server, where it's out of your control and still at risk of being compromised or hacked.

hashtag

hashtag
#coffee from El Salvador is delicious. I know it's the best because I met the producer, visited his biodynamic plantation and witnessed how it was ethically grown, produced and distributed. But with 
hashtag
#blockchain, I wouldn't need to go to this effort or trust the advertising.

Jorge, the plantation owner, talked about how farmers' land has been lost to the banks when they've been underpaid for their produce. So to make his plantation more resilient, he decided to learn biodynamics and permaculture, to sell direct to cafes instead of to the mills, to accept bitcoin and start exporting... 

"We did this to stop borrowing from banks, to pay our workers fairly, be proud of what we grow. This is how we can scale ... we are showing the other farms ... now we sell direct to cafes... no intermediaries, saving money .... the mill trucks have stopped coming as we have nothing for them"

El Salvador adopting bitcoin as legal tender and making the country safe from crime has provided a spark of opportunity.
Jorge wants to use a blockchain business model for supply chain provenance, so businesses and individuals around the world know that the source of their coffee is genuine... ethically and sustainably grown, and of the highest quality (not the chaff). The proof is on the blockchain. 

This entrepreneurial surfacing of tech-for-good... this is how small producers could compete on a level playing field with 'big food' and empower their rural communities, pay fruitpickers well etc.

My family were fruit & veg farmers and Jorge's spirit gives me hope for growers in rural Australia who are as curious to learn.

It also motivates me to keep being an accountant to help businesses and lead other accountants to learn Bitcoin.

A common question I'm asked lately is "Do you still want to specialise in crypto and blockchain after everything that's happened with FTX?

My answer is always the same: "Absolutely."

Why?

To me, nothing has changed. Bitcoin is completely unaffected, other than its fiat-denominated price. New blocks continue to be added to the blockchain every 10 minutes. New users keep joining the network. The code is unchanged. There's no CEO, board of directors, or C-suite to defraud anyone.

Bitcoin's running *exactly* as it was designed to, and has done so since 2009. In a society where 8 billion people sit on their hands every couple of months to wait and hear what an old, unelected man in a suit dictates the cost of money will be, Bitcoin is certainty and stability in a world full of unknowns.

While I'm sad to see many losing their jobs in the industry, I'm as bullish as ever for the future of bitcoin. I'm excited to look for my new clients and accounting partners who'll let me continue my mission to support enterprises that will decentralise and democratise finance.

Had to laugh at this because I am the most painful client for graphic & web designers... I pivoted from art to accounting in my mid 20's and do all my own design work. But sometimes I need technical help... the poor designer has to translate my Canva fantasies and let me brainstorm content straight onto their design.

Someone please give me this value proposition so I can take a step back and let YOU just get the job done for me.... please! And if I get over-involved, I value your time and enjoy the collaboration... so bill me for it!

But remember... as your creative industries accountant... this goes both ways. STOP DOING YOUR OWN BOOKKEEPING really. I mean it. Let my team manage your chart of accounts, double-entry allocations, automations and cost accounting each month.

Because your efforts to save on fees make our job harder ...and you keep asking us to how to do things that took us over 10 years to learn ...and that's why, for you, we're "too expensive" :)

Designers do designing.
Bookkeepers do bookkeeping.
Stay in your lane or pay for the ride.

Our marketing strategy for this industry couldn't be simpler! The message here is "If you DON'T hire an accounting team, you could "misaccount" for  $8bn... but if you DO hire an accounting team, you'll notice that your debits don"t equal your credits."

We do realise that Sam Bankman Fraud was no dummie... being the physics and maths educated son of two lawyers, in a culture that protects white collar criminals and claps for them... he wouldn't have wanted.good accountants getting in his way. 

Nevertheless, to restore public trust, other crypto companies should be boasting about what great accountants and bookkeepers they have! We can name our prices. ð¤

What do you think ... a gold tick on their Web pages like the heart foundation healthy tick on food? ð

Has your accounting practice worked out this value proposition for clients who insist on doing their own bookkeeping to save money? ð 

Tomorrow we're employing another bookkeeper. Who else here is merging accounting and bookkeeping?

Last month I visited El Salvador to research #bitcoin adoption, focusing on regional traders, entrepreneurship and education.

Last year when the El Salvador government made Bitcoin legal tender, they issued a purpose-built Chivo Wallet, a phone app for Bitcoin Lightning payments, with USD$30 worth of Bitcoin granted to every citizen who downloaded the wallet. 

But there wasn't much public education with this Chivo wallet rollout, I found out. So adoption has been slow particularly in non-urban areas where the largely unbanked population use paper USDs and don't understand or trust digital money yet.

Bitcoin circular economies are created when a trader's customers and suppliers are spending/receiving bitcoin. This is being achieved in tourism areas. If a trader's suppliers only accept dollars, then that trader will want their customers to pay in dollars. But what if the customer only wants to pay bitcoin? They may shop elsewhere... ð

So accepting bitcoin is a competitive advantage for traders in locations where people are getting paid in bitcoin and want to spend it (like me in Agnes Water/1770 Australia... but that's my next post)

Some bitcoin advocates encourage El Salvadorans to use Lightning wallets other than the government one. But a Chivo wallet advantage is it enables you to exchange your Bitcoin for USD on the app... 

This in-app exchange facility is great for traders managing cash flow ...they can avoid price volatility and pay their business expenses in USD ...while leaving a balance in bitcoin as savings, protected from currency debasement, to grow their wealth (in a separate cold wallet, ideally). 

Also, the local bitcoin ATMs let them scan their Lightning wallets and withdraw US dollar notes. 

So, with the available tooling, there is no real obstacle to businesses adopting bitcoin other than education. 

Do you know?:
Which Lightning payment apps, other than Chivo, can a trader use to accept bitcoin payments, that have a built-in exchange-to-fiat facility?

What is Digital 3? (RMIT University media release)
RMIT has officially launched its flagship digital economy business school ‘Digital3’ which incorporates world leading research centres including the Blockchain Innovation Hub. 

Blockchain Innovation Hub Co Director, Jason Potts, provided a keynote address at the launch which encapsulated why this model would be critical to the economy in the current tech revolution. 

“This revolution is due to a supercluster of digital tech, sometimes called web3, but it's broader than that.… it is the refactoring and upgrade of the economic operating system.” Read Jason’s complete speech here.
 
First D3 course dropped & available to study FREE online
As part of the launch, Digital3 (D3) dropped an exciting and ‘first of its kind’ web3 business course called ‘Doing Business in Web3’ which is now available to study online at no cost.*

With a duration of six weeks, the online course takes participants through a carefully crafted journey covering diverse topics including strategy, governance, law, token economics and scaling. 

Course developer and Blockchain Innovation Hub Researcher Dr Darcy Allen, says that the course curates the knowledge needed to build business models in web3. “The web3 stack — including blockchains and smart contracts — fundamentally changes how we do business in a digital economy. This course helps to fill that gap in web3 business education.”

The 'Doing Business in web3' course is now available through FutureLearn with a continuous start date so new participants can join and start learning at any time

D3 will continue to roll out online courses and you can register your interest in future courses scheduled to be dropped here."

Startups DIY'ing their own establishment as recommended by government
What exactly is the view held of accountants by AusIndustry? Yesterday I attended Advance Queensland's Regional Innovation Ecosystem Leaders Day in Brisbane.... which was about connecting all our regional hubs in woop-woop so we can help startups access more resources etc. 

I took a lot of positives from the event but there was one negative I had to tackle head-on. 

A Qld Regional Manager for AusIndustry talked about Australian Government Innovation... presenting the wonderful business.gov.au website ð that you can find EVERYTHING you need to start your own business... "without some accountant or some lawyer making you set up the wrong business structure and giving you inappropriate advice that costs you... just go here instead"

Errrrr, I don't think he bargained on the accountants in the room.

I spoke up about how in the last 20 years I have spent more time rectifying mistakes made by founders who have DIY'd their own business startups without advice or any planned budget for it, than I have actually setting them up. More time putting out fires than helping them manage growth. More fees charged to fix sh!t. 

Innovation Hub leaders should be connecting with regional accountants... and developing new business support models with them.... so that founders can have a better shot at not being the 3 out of 4 startups that fail in their first five years.

A few people came up to me afterwards agreeing he was a muppet.

It's one thing for government agencies to tell us there won't be tax returns or BAS work anymore in the next 3-5 years... but competing with us for advisory services?? 

Red flag. Need to address... perhaps through speaking at our innovation hubs. Any suggestions?

Electra Frost created a private online event for ACCOUNTANTS ON-CHAIN - Crypto & Blockchain Accountants and Advisers Group.

#directorsID #decentralisedID #KYC
This is about understanding some of the fringe clients active in the crypto space. There are various freedom movement networks on Telegram warning people about the compulsory Director ID registration required by the ATO. 

Do you get clients asking you if there is any way of avoiding or working around Directors IDs? 

These are my opinions and I'd like to know yours.

It's easy to flip them off as silly or conspiracy theorists ...but if we're educating our clients to be successful in the evolving digital economy, I think we need to be respectful.... and tackle this head on ... encourage more pragmatic and competitive thinking.

We get that they're concerned about government digital overreach etc etc.... but these freedom movements only seem to be fear mongering and leading people into trouble. Not offering new knowledge or strategic options other than "just say no". Like the cash brigade.

"the more you resist, the more it persists"

They're better off learning about decentralised ID and getting behind those projects than trying to fight Director IDs. Learn about the democratising web3 technology being developed that will allow us to legally interact with corporate and government platforms while owning and protecting their personal and business data. 

Thoughts?

Who'd like a session on decentralised identity?

Time Wang, the co-founder of coinstash.com.au wrote on LinkedIn - wanting to see staking rewards and airdrops income be capitalised and GST rollover reliefs... sounds good... not sure what he means about the GST treatment with regard to DCE's though:

"For those of us that trade sometimes we come across situations where the tax outcome seems highly unpractical or even ridiculous. For example, if you were exposed to Luna/UST, chances are your tax bill is now bigger than your entire crypto portfolio.

Consultation on the Board of Taxation Review of the Tax Treatment of Digital Assets and Transactions closes on Friday 30 September 2022.

This is your chance to have a say in what could result in one of the most significant changes to Australian tax law since GST.

There are significant issues with the taxation of digital assets and transactions under current Australian tax law and if Australia is to be a leader in the blockchain and innovation space in the future, the issues must be addressed as a matter of urgency.

If you think this only applies to individuals who dabble in cryptocurrency you are wrong - blockchain is the future, it will impact on entity types, decision making models, income tax, GST, international tax, transfer pricing, business products...

Be a part of the change.

Interested in Central Bank Digital Currencies #CBDCs and how they might work in respect of completely automated taxation and more? 

Look no further - in this article Keir Finlow-Bates present examples of all sorts of different functionality that can be coded up quickly and easily, and explains them in the context of the central banking paradigm.  
 ð¤

After reading it, does it make sense why decentralised digital currencies have more appeal to a growing segment of the global population? We could code a tax collection smart contract into the Decentralised Autonomous Organisations (DAO) that people earn income through... that is, a system of social cooperation rather than of top-down enforcement.

"there are properties and features of cryptocurrencies and tokens that would be highly desirable in existing money systems. Desirable for central banks, that is. Unsurprisingly, it is not the ideas of decentralized management, personal ownership, or privacy and anonymity that are of interest though.

"It is the features pertaining to control. If you have programmable money, then you can code in all sorts of limitations, conditions, oversight, and tracking features."

Enjoying the Sydney buzz of an evening stroll through Darling Harbour, while processing a full day of advisory training.

In the 1980's Warren Buffet identified that a business's intangible assets increase with inflation: “What is more likely is that the Goodwill will increase – in current, if not in constant, dollars – because of inflation". 

At today's Accountants Academy induction workshop we talked about Balance Sheet Thinking...beyond the hard assets, all the intangibles that are not shown on the balance sheet...

 That includes all your business information, processes and innovations that ought to be documented and fully systemised across the organisation. To generate profits and scale upon. 

Now, I realise Mr Buffet isn't a fan of Bitcoin technology... but he may change his mind when incumbent firms are creating NFTs from all this valuable business information that they can securely own in blockchain and that others will pay good crypto to use, in a completely automated Web3 marketplace.

Complete information proprietorship monetised in Web3. That'll be the way I scale.

Ok, we may not know how to do that yet. 
But we can write down all our processes now, create a register, so it's all ready to go. Small steps are the way to big goals

FASB and IASB members discussed whether accounting standard setters should get ahead of regulators in defining how to account for digital assets. Does this pose a risk of the accounting industry endorsing digital assets, which may influence the market? The panel agreed that the better approach would be for standard setting bodies to stay on top of current developments and be prepared to respond appropriately.
Given the broad scope of digital assets, panellists proposed that the sensible approach would be to focus on specific subsets of digital assets as they become more prevalent.

We are pretty sure that staking rewards are taxable income (but not quite certain yet) - but a question that is never answered no matter how much I Google it is...

*WHEN* are staking rewards taxed. The derivation point is much much more complex than it appears. Questions I'll think about all weekend:

Are they taxed when they appear in your wallet?

Are they taxed when you can claim them via a smart contract call?

Is the taxing point when you can actually access them? (think Beacon chain ETH staking)

Does cash and accruals accounting methods impact this?

Does the constructive receipt rule in section 6-5(4) bring forward the derivation time?

Just like when we analysed Anchor, you have to get into the guts of a protocol to understand the tax outcomes. Generalisations at this stage of the crypto tax evolution can very easily lead to bad advice. Also the case law on derivation is scant and fact specific, we are mostly on our own on this one.

The digital economy: 3 things 7-figure 
hashtag
#businessowners know…

That 5-figure business owners still haven’t caught onto yet.

Keep reading to find out more:

Traditional 
hashtag
#banking rails shrink your marketplace: Paying or receiving funds from the western world is pretty easy. The banking connections are set, margins are thin and we are all rightly or wrongly locked in and benefitting from the race to the bottom. 

But for those looking to add value in underserviced parts of the world the game is different. It takes real buying power, a long list of middlemen and local know-how to jump through the long list of 
hashtag
#regulatory and 
hashtag
#custom hoops and still successfully turn a profit. Those that do can then enjoy the wider margins as it is a privilege reserved for the few.

Traditional banking rails are to slow: Even with SWIFT and corresponding banks, businesses are forced to accept 2-3 working days for funds to become available. This bottleneck in cash flow poses numerous risks but the main one is a scary thought. Imagine if your direct competitors could get access to their funds 25% quicker than you could, consistently. How long would it take them to maximise a competitive advantage? How much more demand could they create? How much more inventory could they take on and respond to that demand faster?

Suppliers make concessions to those who make it easier: Businesses in emerging markets have switched to crypto. This trend continues to grow as it allows them to level the playing field in an ultra-competitive global market. Western businesses have started to recognise this. They decide to pay said suppliers in crypto to make their lives easier in exchange for a discount on the services provided. Win/Win.


So to recap, this is what you need to remember:

A standard currency account will not carry you far in the digital world. Open your businesses up to blockchain, make the most of the speed and efficiency it can provide and let your team sell into a broader market.

To play globally you have to think outside the box: traditional banking rails stop this.
Speed kills: Cashflow is king and whilst it is stuck in the pipes you cannot put it to work.
Suppliers/clients will do business with whoever makes it easier: 
hashtag
#Crypto is becoming more and more popular in emerging markets, you can no longer rely on just a traditional bank account.

Hey Brainies. Do you offer webinar training to your clients, or do you have an idea?

This memory popped up in my feed today, and I thought it might inspire others to do client webinars. Maybe on how to to get their info ready before they come in for tax? It doesn't have to be too serious and professional. You just need Canva to make pretty slides and invite your clients to a lunch meetup. It can be free. We charged for it.

 In the middle of 2020, we came up with this fun monthly webinar to teach our sole trader clients how to reconcile Xero monthly, do their own BASs and claim their own JobKeeper. It was a crazy time... and we had too many clients to help... so this is how we bulk-helped them. The program only went for 4 months (because I sold my practice) but it was fun for the staff, boosted their confidence, and we got a lot of appreciation for it.

Wondering why your clients have so many wallets? They may be protecting their assets. $5M of Solana-based tokens was just drained from 8000 wallets! Funds have also been drained without knowledge or approval from user's hot wallets such as Phantom, Slope and TrustWallet. The attacks are still in play and it's never been more important to move assets to a cold wallet and secure those private keys.

Bitcoin is capped at 21 million units so how can it replace money? It's not enough for billions of people. Correct! A monetary system must accommodate global demand for currency units. Hence, the #SAT 

The #fiat solution: create more fiat units --> results in a loss of purchasing power until the currency collapses. By design, inflation is guaranteed, what is variable is the rate of inflation, the Consumer Price Index (CPI.) The CPI is calculated by the increasing or decreasing prices of the government-selected (btw, continuously changing) consumer basket of goods to determine the official inflation number. The government aims at debasing the money at a 2% rate. Why 2%? Because at this rate of debasement (inflation), people don't protest on the streets, and politicians can still get elected or re-elected. With fiat, saving is a losing proposition that necessitates making investments (inherently risky) in an attempt to outpace inflation.

The #bitcoin proposition: create a fixed number of SATs when more SATs are needed then divide them --> resulting in a gain of purchasing power, forever! Once you earn your money, your bitcoin, you can store it for the future knowing that it will give you more purchasing power. Today, since 98% of the world still has NOT discovered the power of bitcoin, it is necessary to hold it for 200 Weeks. Meaning, hold your bitcoin for 3.8 years and you will have added to your net worth. With bitcoin, saving is a winning proposition where there's no need to make any investments.

Don't get confused.

The total number of SATS will never change, there will only be 20999999.97690000 x 100M SATs, PERIOD.

Now, perhaps when the need arises to further subdivide a sat, say in 100 years, 200 years, we could call them #nakomotos or #NAKs where 1 SAT = 100M NAKs. Why 100M NAKs? To honor Satoshi Nakamoto, the inventor of Bitcoin.

"Sometimes the most important things in life are the most obvious. You see what we are doing with blockchain, now called web3 or âmetaverse as a token economyâ is.... trumpets blaring â¦ creating decentralised, censorship resistant, digital, and programmable money for internet payments, just like what we do today with cash or paper money.

 So why is that a big deal? A super-big deal? Well, there is only three things we need to get right for the new internet:

1.    Who controls your identity on the internet?

2.    Who controls what you own on the internet?

1.    Your identity is owned by Google, Amazon, Meta or Apple

3.    You become a digital serf â because you gave anyway your data for âfreeâ services

I donât want to become a digital serf. Do you? 

So yes, internet money is a big deal. I will be talking about what I think this means to the new digital citizen and the enterprises that serve these new, empowered digital customers."

Tax practitioners based in Australia: Here's an easy opportunity to have your say on blockchain and tax compliance!

If you have dealt with at least one client who has undertaken blockchain-related activities (such as traded/invested in cryptoassets etc.), you're invited to have your say by participating in a short 15-minute on-line survey by Dr Elizabeth Morton (RMIT).
https://rmit.au1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_3HSYwy9VHonOdIG?jfefe=new

It has become evident that taxpayers are increasingly participating in blockchain technology. As a result, there has been an increasing interest in the implications for tax compliance, including the impact and challenges of these activities on tax practitioners.

In 2021, the Senate Select Committee on Australia as a Technology and Financial Centre Final Report flagged recommendations concerning Blockchain activities with regards to the CGT implications as well as the need for ATO guidance to be more up to date. Whilst the Government noted the recommendation, it gave the Treasury the task of working with the Board of Taxation to undertake a broader review of the appropriate framework for taxing digital transactions and assets. In March 2022, the Board released its terms of reference for its review to be provided to the Treasurer by the end of the year.

In this regard, research is being undertaken by Dr Elizabeth Morton of RMIT University, as well as Assoc Prof Gillian Vesty (RMIT University), Dr Lan Nguyen (RMIT University) and Assoc Prof Ken Devos (Swinburne University), on the impact and challenges of blockchain-related activities on tax practitioners in Australia. The purpose of this project is to examine the blockchain-related activities with respect to tax compliance.

Current anecdotal evidence suggests that further technical and theoretical skills are required to support blockchain understanding and tax practices. Consequently, the empirical evidence that will be collected through this project will greatly assist in identifying this potential tax practitioner skillâs gap, while also contributing to the consultation processes with government.

Participation in this research project is voluntary and anonymous.

How to complete the survey: You are invited to take part in a short 15-minute survey by clicking on the link below to express your views on the implications of blockchain technology on public practice and tax compliance:
https://rmit.au1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_3HSYwy9VHonOdIG?jfefe=new

If you would like further information on this project, please contact:
Dr. Elizabeth Morton, co-investigator, +61 3 9925 5886, elizabeth.morton@rmit.edu.au
All correspondence will be treated confidentially.

Bull markets incite greed and bring about opportunities to leverage by borrowing against current crypto assets to buy more crypto.

Here's one for the #managementaccountants with clients #mining . How much it costs #miners to produce each #Bitcoin in electricity or whatever their energy source of choice is, greatly impacts profit margins. The next major factor is the price each BTC is currently trading at.

When the cost to produce each Bitcoin is higher than the assetâs price, thereâs a higher chance that miners will sell their BTC supply to fund operations or as they cash out and capitulate.

A tool has been developed that measures the current cost of production and layers it over Bitcoin price action. This tool is aptly named the Cost of Production indicator.

The future of #ecommerce
Telling the machine what you want, earning cryptocurrency rewards, used to buy NFTs in the retailer's metaverse mall, browsing via VR headset, and using 3D body scan data to try clothes on. I wonder how the Food Court works?
.................

From Panagiotis Kriaris (LinkedIn):
The meteoric rise of ecommerce over the past years has profoundly changed not only how consumers shop but also the way businesses are run. Yet, the degree of change so far is only a fraction of what is coming. Letâs take a look.
 
Start with the numbers:

â     There are over 2.14 billion global online shoppers and between 12 to 24 million online shops (according to digital intheround, 2021 figures)

â     The global ecommerce industry is forecast to grow by almost $11 trillion between 2021 and 2025 with an annualized growth rate (CAGR in industry jargon) of almost 29%, according to Report Linker

â     Global ecommerce sales will reach $6.4 trillion by 2024 according to Statista
 
Customers today have at their disposal more ways than ever to discover, engage with and evaluate products and a wider - than ever before - array of digital and physical venues (or their combination) to buy them.
 
At the heart of the disruption so far, we find four fundamental shifts:
 
â    A behavioral shift towards digital, mobile-first experiences, accelerated (but not initiated) by the pandemic 
 
â    A transition from products to experiences with a complete overhaul of the customer journey
 
â    The hyper-personalization and frictionlessness of experiences have taken their place not as a nice-to-have but as a requirement in an omnichannel world where the shift from digital to physical interactions and vice-versa is the new norm
 
â    technology acting as a key innovation enabler and disruption driver
 
Despite the global appeal and application of these four shifts, there are still strong regional and local variations when it comes to payments, the lifeblood of e-commerce: for example, Chinese shoppers use mostly e-wallets (61%) whereas Americans still mainly use credit cards (38%) â based on data from digital intheround.
 
Going forward, the future of e-commerce will be dominated by technology driving already existing trends in a much broader context. Examples abound:
 
â   artificial intelligence (AI), Augmented Reality (AR) and 3D assistants driving personalization to new avenues (Amazonâs Room Decorator tool provides a glimpse to the future)
 
â    Metaverse potentially revolutionizing shopping by means of introducing new meta markets
 
â    The internet of things  driving customer retention and efficiency gains
 
As e-commerce matures and grows, it will be making its way to an experience-first, immersive next-gen phase, bringing a (welcome) shift of power toward the consumer and inevitably a drastic shake-up of the value chain as a result of increased competition.

Central Bank Digital Currency
 
The European Central Bank (ECB) has released a paper on digital economy, privacy and central bank digital currency (CBDC).
 
The ongoing digitalization of the economy has profound implications for the way payments are settled with declining use of cash, increased online transactions using electronic or digital payments and privately issued digital currencies.
 
These developments have led to a debate among policy makers about the potential creation of CBDC.
 
Electronic payments create vast amounts of data, so data privacy is one motivation for such a change to our monetary system. While more data can in principle enable better products and services, economists have become increasingly concerned about the anti‐ competitive effects of large data monopolies in the form of dominant digital platforms.
 
Broadly speaking, digital public money in the form of a CBDC may have a comparative advantage at providing privacy because, unlike private sector alternatives, it is not subject to profit‐maximization incentives.
 
This paper speaks to this debate by developing a model of financial intermediation, payment choice, and privacy in the digital economy.
 
Cash preserves anonymity but cannot be used for more efficient online transactions. By contrast, bank deposits can be used online but do not preserve anonymity. Banks use the information contained in deposit flows to charge merchants in need of financing. Payment tokens issued by digital platforms allow merchants to hide information from payment flows from banks but enable platforms to stifle competition by keeping merchants locked in a “walled garden”.
 
An independent digital payment instrument such as a CBDC that allows agents to share their payment data with selected parties can overcome all frictions and achieves the efficient allocation. It also prevents anti‐competitive practices by platforms, further raising efficiency. These results have important policy implications for ECB.

I talk a lot about #Auditchain and am asked "What problem does it solve"? There are so many companies with errors on their financial reports, be it by fraud or negligence. #XBRL is the open international standard for digital business reporting, improving reporting in the public interest. By using XBRL to produce and display reports... instead of PDFs, Excel or html, as we do now... we make accounting information #machinereadable and more useful. Machine readable information is becoming more important. Auditchain makes it super reliable and puts in on a blockchain that cannot be altered or shut down. 

If a system can "read" what's in your ledger against what's on your machine readable report, it can bring to your attention the issues and noone can hide them. To prevent THIS from happening:

YOUR IDEAL CRYPTO CLIENT?

I just got off a thought provoking call with David Bocca, group member who, like me, is letting go some clients to give himself some breathing space to learn, and consider new accounting practice models. That got us to thinking about the kinds of crypto clients we want to work with. 

It's an instant no to any that don't want to pay tax and take advice. So which crypto clients DO we want? We should know before they turn up, right?

The trend is to move away from individuals, and target tech -friendly micro businesses and SME's. Scaled services combining the regular reporting with virtual CFO type work and year-round compliance. I think of this as business management services.

Although individuals increasingly need help with crypto...

In my practice, scaled down to 3 employees and 20 clients, we lead with the bookkeeping so we can do it right with tech, and manage the rest in our workflow. Fees average around $2K a month, but going for higher. 

We're getting ready in 2022, I guess, for the crypto and Web3 businesses that will need us in 2023-24. For $4K+ a month with bookkeeping.

In the meantime, would we do ad-hoc crypto consulting..? I don't know yet. 
Your thoughts?

Electra Frost created a private event for ACCOUNTANTS ON-CHAIN - Crypto & Blockchain Accountants and Advisers Group.

A private group for accountants, bookkeepers and advisers to support each other in keeping pace with rapid developments in bitcoin, crypto and blockchain tech adoption. 

A safe place to ask questions and share ideas - whether it's about dealing with client crypto matters, how blockchain innovation affects our future professional practices, what the ATO should do, how we can best service the growing numbers of investors and SMEs in this space, and more. 

We closely follow regulatory, accounting standards and tax reforms that have been put in motion, new software to make our work easier, and industry events, quality education and CPD. 

This group was set up by Electra Frost (tax agent, FIPA, CTA) and Gordon Christian, co-founders of Digital Playhouse - a social enterprise and registered charity in Central Coastal Qld - on a mission to accelerate digital technology and blockchain learning and adapt skills to emerging opportunities. We believe that crypto-literate accounting professionals are integral to guiding compliant and successful crypto/Web3 businesses for the benefit of the Australian economy.

#braggreport Last October, the Select Committee on Australia as a Technology and Financial Centre chaired by Senator Andrew Bragg made recommendations in the **[Bragg report](https://www.aph.gov.au/Parliamentary_Business/Committees/Senate/Financial_Technology_and_Regulatory_Technology/AusTechFinCentre/Final_report)**, which, if adopted, would reflect a radical departure from the current regulatory environment for cryptocurrency and other digital assets. 

Of the 12 Final Report recommendations, 11 were either noted or accepted by Government. DAOs represent the most significant erosion to the tax base and are being prioritised, and the Board of Taxation terms of reference for their upcoming review of digital assets and digital asset transactions will cover nearly every aspect of tax law - issues from interpretation of existing law and areas for new tax policy. 

There is no likelihood of tax clarity this year, as the BoT report is not due until Dec 2022. So it's unlikely that any crypto tax policy will find its way into the upcoming budget or election platform as the work wonât be complete.

This was Senator Bragg's address to Blockchain Week 2022 on March 21:
https://www.startupdaily.net/2022/03/senator-andrew-bragg-calls-for-a-single-act-for-the-australian-government-to-regulate-crypto-and-blockchain/

#internationaltax  I had a chat with Harrison Dell, Sydney-based Tax Lawyer specialising in Crypto, #DAO and #NFT About how challenging the international tax is for DAOs (Decentralised Autonomous Organisations) that are popping up now. JOIN HIS DISCORD CHANNEL - it's a great place to watch him and other crypto tax-pros in action answering curly questions! https://discord.gg/KAeK9QATcw

DAOs are like the vehicles for De-Fi, in a blockchain based business model and structure, that can have any number of members located in various unknown jurisdictions. It's a moving feast and being wholly compliant really isn't possible. 

Hopefully, Recommendation No. 4 of the Bragg Report (The Government establish a new Decentralised Autonomous Organisation company structure.) will be acted on soon. I think we need a consistent global approach, because updating DTAs will take forever 

DAOs operate âon decentralised blockchain infrastructure, whose operations are pre-determined in open source code and enforced through smart contractsâ. This means they donât fit neatly within Australiaâs existing company structures. The committee suggests that the resultant regulatory uncertainty âis preventing the establishment of projects of significant scale in Australiaâ. It takes the view that implementing a DAO company structure will âdrive economic activity in this space and be a magnet for Australian innovation for DAOs, driving local jobs and tax revenueâ.

While so much of the current tech innovation is experimental, it's a good time to develop our understanding of blockchain concepts with sessions like this. So we can contribute our experience to real projects in future.  #blockchaineducation #futureofbusiness

Today's Blockchain Week Tax Roundtable, moderated by tax lawyer Joni Pirovich, with the Senate Committee on Technology and Finance, scrutinised what tax principles might be fit for Web3 based on the panel's experience of applying tax laws to new and emerging crypto applications. The ATO and Board of Taxation are in no hurry to clarify their positions. 

More taxpayers are being encouraged in the meantime to use tax agents for 'safeharbour'. So it's now up to us to gauge the reasonable care that'll be expected of us with clients' Web3 and DeFi activities. Perhaps we need solid guidelines around competency requirements.

Tomorrow I will be speaking on the Brisbane Blockchain Week IRL tax panel with Nick from Kova Tax and Rafael from Tax on Chain about how our industry can prepare and be supported. I've been brainstorming with Craig Wood and we have a few ideas to advocate for Tax & BAS agents. Hope you can watch and add your views afterwards ð
