It's important to source from a trusted official distributor. Cheaper devices on eBay etc can have viruses. You could get the less costly Ledger Nano S but it holds less crypto and can be a little harder to navigate with.   Q: Why should I learn by doing this?  A:

These devices serve as a potentially invaluable insurance. Our crypto clients are being their own banks, and that comes with a high degree of responsibility. If your client kept their life savings under their mattress you'd have something to say.

 If they keep their crypto in an online wallet and their private keys on their phone, it's critical!  The main advantage of hardware wallets is they're widely considered to provide the most secure storage option for cryptocurrency and tokens. 

Unlike web-based wallets and desktop software wallets, which are exposed to risks like hacking, malware and phishing scams, hardware wallets like the Ledger Nano X provide secure offline storage for many digital assets.   

Most importantly, a backup of your private keys on something more durable than paper is essential for long term security of high value assets. 

The Billfodl is a small, wallet sized stainless steel enclosure designed to house any kind of crypto private key or recovery seed you may need to protect from physical threats of destruction.  Please type any questions as a comment on this thread.

As a creative industries accountant since 2000, there's no question about it, I have to ride this wave! Creatives like both money and social goodness, I've experienced. They especially like finding ways to make NEW money to keep doing good... sweet passive income... now we're talking crypto. Seeing huge opportunities for my clients and friends, it's such a blast learning about Australian blockchain innovations. It pushed me to get qualified with Blockchain Collective in 2022, so we can formulate better prospects for creative businesses this decade.

Check out the Creative Industries Faculty at QUT (Queensland University of Technology) (article below) - they developed a world-class blockchain prototype system for digital rights management and royalty distribution, opening the door for small publishers to new commercial opportunities. Micropayments are triggered via smart contracts to all creative professionals involved in the writing and publishing process, with all purchases tracked with a custom digital ledger. Brisbane micropublisher Sue Wright's project, Tiny Owl Workshop, is even creating 'extra' intellectual property using this technology! (love the grassroots initiatives)

Here's the thing -
Involving such new, very technical and complex processes, you see that all collaborating specialists - from IT, coding, legal, accounting, business advisory and the creatives themselves - we all need to learn about applied blockchain, game theory and tokenomics. To conceive and build out these beautiful automated systems that livelihoods will depend on, we begin by understanding the main architecture for structuring a sustainable distributed economy.

Seems daunting but it's OK, we're learning from each other because it all really is so new and coming at a crazy pace. One way is *learning by doing*, even just a little bit at a time... as early adopters I think we can influence how future applications will create and distribute economic value in line with our human values. 

The blockchain community is full of inspiring people who see a better world ahead, creative thinkers, welcoming and encouraging, some have been very kind to me. We pay it forward.

My door is open to curious colleagues :) 

I was just sharing this article, and felt more to say, so excited about 2022!

I need help, Brainies. New client has just signed the contract to buy a new business to merge with his existing business! Needs the ABN ASAP. He's late 60's and is bringing son into the business so he can retire in 5 years. Has done no estate planning, and gets a pension.

- His existing company with his wife is carrying $140K capital losses, $150 tax losses and a $60K bank loan. 
- Has pulled $100K from wife's super to buy new business (goodwill, plant & stock) with... this will be turned into a loan or convertible note perhaps.
- The new business is a great prospect, estimate $100K first year profit (after directors paid)

He wants a family trust "to protect business from son's future wife if they divorce" (not my scope, but OK). He's prepared to walk away from the old company's losses but likes the idea of using up the losses to save tax. 

Would you set up a new operating company with a family trust owning the shares? If the old company was in the ownership mix, it wouldn't make use of franking credits. 

But if old company was a shareholder, it could sell its shares to the family trust in a few year's time, to get profits out and use up its capital losses. 

(I feel some Part IVA discomfort)

He also suggested his old current company's business could be sold to the new one for $45K (looks plausible). By related-party loan.

BUT if we were to keep it simple and buy the new business with his old company, the losses would be used up with profits. 

Could we add a family trust shareholder to the existing company, before it buys the new business? Fails continuity of ownership, but i think it will pass the new 'similar business test' as his existing business activities will carry on in a primary way, just with retail added-on. 

I am a little out of my depth and under pressure. Happy to pay for some expert navigation on this. :)

El Salvador could not have made BTC legal tender without the Lightning Network (network 2) scalability.

"The Lightning Network is a decentralized scaling solution for Bitcoin that abstracts small transactions between users away from the Bitcoin blockchain. It utilizes smart contracts to settle the final balance of those payments into the Bitcoin âbase layerâ while ensuring that all transactions abide by the rules of the Bitcoin protocol.

Lightning payments are thus not recorded on the Bitcoin blockchain â only transactions that either open or close a channel are. By not requiring the transaction fees or wait times that on-chain Bitcoin transactions typically do, Lightning enables its users to enjoy affordable, fast and private payments."

https://www.nasdaq.com/articles/lightning-network-capacity-breaks-1500-btc-2021-06-17

Financial literacy in the digital age begins with being able to distinguish between centralised and decentralised purpose.

This article is from the standpoint of centralised authority on issues of identity and data in cashless transacting with individuals. The innovations in this area are being driven by blockchain (Permissionless, Trustless, Decentralised Identity Systems) but the Centralised Authorities are not pursuing public and control-less systems of self-governance. Theirs is a different blockchain use case for digital identity, data and monetary capture.

Be alert to double-speak when hearing about "protections" to the end-user when the real intention is to control and profit from the end-user.

Do you know about the School of Arts movement of the 19th century? In the spirit of enlightenment these grand structures were built in our towns, for public lectures and diffusion of knowledge and skills. Out of these humble voluntary operations developed the local public library, the modern neighbourhood centre, and formal systems of adult and technical education.

"Underpinning the movement were the ideas that industry and society would benefit from a scientifically educated artisan class and that a new breed of inventors would arise from this class."

Now, in this digital age of converging technologies and the Fourth Industrial Revolution, transforming all our jobs and business models, should we repeat history with "Schools of the Future"?

#filmreinvestment #producertaxoffset
These reinvestment deals that suppliers to film producers are asked to sign drive me crazy. Have you come across them?

In a nutshell, some film producers qualify for the 40% tax offset by inflating their eligible expenditure to over $500,000 through special deals with their suppliers whereby 50% or more of the supplier's invoice is applied as an investment in the film. The so-called reinvestments are highly speculative, because low budget films rarely show profits. But suppliers are told (by the producer's lawyer) that a "good accountant" can help the supplier write off that amount for tax.

Look. No matter how you slice it, no matter how fancily worded your contract is, if your service is $100K plus GST and you only receive $40K, you're deemed to have been paid $100K for tax and GST purposes. At tax time, the supplier gets a shock when Subsection 6-5(4) of the ITAA 1997 is explained to them. 
As for writing off the $60K that was forfeited as a reinvestment - it's capital - if you have a capital loss when your interest or rights are "written off" in future, then your capital loss is quarantined. No tax benefit until you have a capital gain to offset, right?

What I am missing that the "good accountants" know? You can't write off a capital loss against income. Can you say that the investment was just so high risk that it wasn't an investment, after all, it was just an ordinary business expense (because this is how all the films are funded in Australia, it's a regular thing). 

Devil's advocate here. It sounds like a scheme, promoted to get the producer tax offset. 

I am happy to be proven wrong but has anyone seen a ruling on this arrangement, done a special way?

My response to indignant supplier client is "Sure, let's get ATO early engagement on this, apply for a private ruling... show me your contract". But there is no contract - he wants a tax answer first, and then he will pay our entertainment industry lawyer friend for a contract - and the producer needs to know NOW if he's in. Other accountants are doing it, I am told. I haven't seen any published private rulings lately.... maybe I need to look harder. ???

....
The qualifying expenditure threshold for Producer Tax Offset will increase to $1mil in July so we might see less of these in future.

Take a day off, you say? It’s not possible to take a day off when there’s so much to keep up with every week, constant changes in legislation, rules and ATO guides ... that still don’t answer the clients’ questions. We must keep up every time there’s a whole lot of new info, every week/day/hour because if we give wrong advice then we may be responsible for mistakes and misinterpretations. This really isn’t a time we want to cause a client in difficulty to miss out on much needed money, or cause an ATO audit of their JobKeeper entitlement after they spent it or... heaven forbid... be liable under the Crimes Act for a PAYG withholding irregularity increasing their CashFlow Boost.

# Client-ATO dealings that undermine your advice 

Sometimes itâs like being a lawyer whose clients dob themselves into the police.  Do. Not. Call. The. ATO. 

Please contribute - Iâm writing an article for LinkedIn and client newsletter, to get our view heard on this, and really need your anecdotes and opinions to fill it out! (will be anonymous and Iâll be happy for others to share it)

How do you convince your clients to trust you and to not contact the ATO directly? To not freak out when they get a robo-letter with outdated details or MyGov shows something odd? To give you the benefit of the doubt before reacting? To understand the value of representation?

Increasingly, clients seem to think the ATO know everything and the ATO website is tax law... and are calling the ATO for advice and searching online for answers instead of calling us. Then coming back to us telling us weâre wrong, or drawing unnecessary attention to themselves. 

The automatically generated letters and emails are taken personally and weâre chastised for them. 

I amended a clientâs BAS (refund) and the poorly worded ATO letter followed saying she now owed $12K (was previously $13K). It sent her into a keyboard warrior tailspin from which she couldnât recover, and she had to find a new accountant who wouldnât cause such shocking letters in the first place.

I registered a client for GST (required) and after his first BAS refund the ATO sent him an email querying whether he should be registered for GST (???). As a result, the client is convinced I was wrong to register him and duped him into it to make more money out of him. Heâs going to run into trouble. Subsequent calls between him and the ATO have further confused matters to the point where Iâve called them to ask them to refer him back to me instead if he calls in future. They canât, apparently. They have to give taxpayers misleading general advice if they call asking for it.

I have many other examples which Iâm sure others here would relate to. Most people are OK, but those volatile and less bright clients who donât understand and create a drama can do some real damage to your morale and reputation. Ditching can be difficult. Can we get through to them?

What do you do when an ex client owes you $200 in Xero fees on the subscription you manage, didnât accept a transfer a few months ago, isnât paying your Xero fees invoice or replying, and the new accountant (theyâve added as a user, is logging in) hasnât even sent an ethical letter? Remove them all as users and use it as leverage to get paid...., or just cancel the subscription and let them sort it out with Xero?

Glad to see the back of them and just donât want any more time or money wasted on them, really.

Including capital gains schedules in tax returns for full main residence exemptions. Do you do it?

I've previously read on the ATO website that a capital gain doesn't have to be declared in a tax return if eligible for the full exemption. I've disputed it with clients many times over the years. You need to declare the capital gain event in any case, and your election to claim the main residence exemption is evidenced in the tax return. Otherwise a taxpayer is leaving themselves wide open for an ATO audit.

Most clients take me seriously and pay our extra fee for a CGT schedule and MRE election document. Some don't want to pay and walk.

Practice Ignition convert here! 
Who else is charging upfront for tax return jobs? If not, please share why not?

At first, I thought "no way" when PI recommended upfront payment for most jobs, unless on a monthly plan.

But now with 15 May approaching, I am loving our new PI procedure. We have too many late sole trader clients with CGT etc, complex returns. Basically - You pay upfront, we will automatically prioritise your tax return before the deadline. If you don't want to pay upfront, we can do it after 15 May, no stress. 

We also raised our fees now... kind of like Uber surge pricing.

So far, the ones who've left us have actually given me a great feeling of relief. They always do their returns late and complain about fees, and are not worth the stress anymore. 

This thing is saving us so much time on invoicing and workflow management, so we can focus on work, it's worth losing those clients over.

Hopefully, HOPEFULLY, this will also get them in before March in future. We need to be focused on advisory work and business development now, not tax returns.

Would love to hear how others are using PI. :)

What's the go with the ATO taking it upon themselves to contact clients directly to cancel their GST registrations? Has anyone else heard from a client about this?

Under the GST Act, registration can be voluntary. The real issue is whether someone is entitled to an ABN. 

We registered a sole trader for GST 6 months ago, with substantial turnover from a 2 year old business. Last BAS reported sales well over the threshold. 

He just received a phone call and a bizarre email from the ATO as follows (i.e. they bypassed us, the tax agent and contact):

=========================
We need to talk to you about your GST registration, to confirm you are entitled to it.
 
To work this out we may ask you questions about:
âº what business activities you carry out
âº how often you do these activities
âº the type and value of supplies you make
âº your estimated annual turnover.
 
Phone us by 9 November, 2018 otherwise we may cancel your GST registration.
=================================

I called the ATO for him and drilled them for bypassing us, asking what the campaign was called and its purpose. I couldn't get anything out of them but want to register a complaint to the ATO. If a tax agent determines that a client should register for GST and is lodging BAS's, there should be no question about entitlement... ? Bizarre.
